key_facts: Flows parallel to isobars
Low pressure to the RIGHT, high pressure to the LEFT
Anticlockwise around SH low-pressure systems
Clockwise around SH high-pressure systems
Speed increases with closer isobar spacing
Best approximation of wind above the friction layer

syllabus_desc: Describe the 'gradient wind' in the Southern Hemisphere with respect: anticyclonically curved isobars.
concept: Around an SH anticyclone (clockwise flow), the centrifugal force acts outward from the centre — the same direction as the PGF. The Coriolis force must balance both PGF and centrifugal force. Therefore the gradient wind around an anticyclone is FASTER than the geostrophic wind for the same isobar spacing.
exam_tip: Gradient wind around SH anticyclone > geostrophic speed. Centrifugal + PGF both outward; Coriolis must balance both.
key_facts: Anticyclone = clockwise flow in SH
Centrifugal force acts outward (same direction as PGF)
Coriolis must balance PGF + centrifugal force combined
Gradient wind speed GREATER than geostrophic for same isobar spacing

syllabus_desc: Describe the 'gradient wind' in the Southern Hemisphere with respect: cyclonically curved isobars.
concept: Around an SH cyclone (anticlockwise flow), the centrifugal force acts outward — the same direction as the Coriolis force and opposing the PGF. Only part of the Coriolis force balances the PGF. Therefore the gradient wind around a cyclone is SLOWER than the geostrophic wind for the same isobar spacing.
exam_tip: Gradient wind around SH cyclone < geostrophic speed. Centrifugal acts with Coriolis against PGF — less Coriolis available to balance PGF.
key_facts: Cyclone = anticlockwise flow in SH
Centrifugal force acts outward (same direction as Coriolis)
Centrifugal + Coriolis partially cancel PGF
Gradient wind speed LESS than geostrophic for same isobar spacing
Explains why winds around lows are lighter than isobar spacing alone suggests

syllabus_desc: Describe the 'frictional wind balance'.
concept: Below the friction layer (~2,000 ft AGL), surface friction slows the wind. Reduced speed → reduced Coriolis force → the PGF is no longer balanced → wind backs (turns anticlockwise in SH) toward low pressure and crosses the isobars. The result is a three-way balance: PGF, Coriolis, and friction. The cross-isobar deflection is ~10–15° over the sea, 20–30° over flat terrain, 40–50° over mountains. This cross-isobar flow drives convergence into lows and divergence from highs.
exam_tip: Friction slows wind → less Coriolis → wind backs toward low pressure. Deflection: sea 10–15°, flat 20–30°, mountains 40–50°.
key_facts: Friction reduces wind speed below ~2,000 ft
Less speed = less Coriolis force
Wind backs (anticlockwise SH) toward low pressure
Three-way balance: PGF + Coriolis + friction
Sea: ~10–15° deflection; flat terrain: ~20–30°; mountains: ~40–50°
Cross-isobar flow drives surface convergence into lows

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over: the sea.
concept: Over the sea the surface is smooth and friction is low. Wind direction deflects approximately 10–15° toward low pressure from the geostrophic direction.
exam_tip: Sea: ~10–15° deflection toward low pressure. Smooth surface = low friction.
key_facts: Low friction over sea
Deflection: ~10–15° toward low pressure
Closest to geostrophic of all surface types

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over: flat to undulating ground.
concept: Over flat to undulating ground friction is moderate. Wind deflects approximately 20–30° toward low pressure from the geostrophic direction.
exam_tip: Flat to undulating: ~20–30° deflection toward low pressure.
key_facts: Moderate friction over flat to undulating terrain
Deflection: ~20–30° toward low pressure
Greater than sea, less than mountainous

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over: mountainous regions.
concept: Over mountainous terrain friction is high due to physical obstruction and channelling. Wind deflects approximately 40–50° toward low pressure from the geostrophic direction.
exam_tip: Mountainous: ~40–50° deflection toward low pressure. Highest deflection of all surface types.
key_facts: High friction over mountains
Deflection: ~40–50° toward low pressure
Greatest deflection of all surface types
Wind highly variable in mountains

syllabus_desc: Explain how the following affect the depth of the friction layer: atmospheric stability.
concept: Unstable atmosphere promotes vertical mixing (thermals/convection) which carries frictional effects higher, deepening the friction layer. Stable atmosphere suppresses vertical mixing — the friction layer is shallow.
exam_tip: Unstable = deep friction layer (vertical mixing). Stable = shallow friction layer.
key_facts: Unstable = deeper friction layer
Stable = shallower friction layer
Instability promotes vertical mixing extending friction effects upward
Stability suppresses vertical mixing

syllabus_desc: Explain how the following affect the depth of the friction layer: wind strength.
concept: Stronger winds generate more mechanical turbulence (wind shear between surface and free air), mixing momentum vertically and deepening the friction layer. Light winds produce less mechanical turbulence and a shallower friction layer.
exam_tip: Stronger wind = deeper friction layer (more mechanical turbulence). Light wind = shallower.
key_facts: Stronger wind = deeper friction layer
Mechanical turbulence increases with wind speed
Light wind = shallower friction layer
Wind shear drives vertical mixing

syllabus_desc: Explain how the following affect the depth of the friction layer: surface roughness.
concept: Rough surfaces (buildings, forests, mountains) create more mechanical turbulence, deepening the friction layer. Smooth surfaces (sea, ice) create less friction and result in a shallower friction layer.
exam_tip: Rough surface = deep friction layer. Smooth surface = shallow friction layer.
key_facts: Rough surface = deeper friction layer
Smooth surface = shallower friction layer
Examples rough: forest, city, mountains
Examples smooth: sea, ice, flat plains

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: wave-lengths.
concept: Mountain waves are stationary atmospheric waves that form in the lee of a mountain barrier when stable air flows over it. The wave-length is the horizontal distance between successive wave crests. Typical range: 5–35 km, depending on wind speed and stability. Stronger wind and more stable air produce longer wave-lengths.
exam_tip: Wave-length = horizontal crest-to-crest distance. Typical: 5–35 km. Longer with stronger wind and more stable air.
key_facts: Wave-length = horizontal distance between wave crests
Typical range: 5–35 km
Increases with wind speed and atmospheric stability
Stationary waves in the lee of the mountain barrier

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: position and rotation of any possible rotor zones.
concept: Rotor zones form beneath the first wave crest downwind of the ridge, at approximately mountain peak level or below. The air in a rotor circulates in a closed vortex — surface flow reverses toward the mountain (upwind). This creates extreme turbulence and wind shear.
exam_tip: Rotor: beneath first wave crest downwind, ~mountain peak level. Surface flow reverses toward mountain. Extreme turbulence.
key_facts: Located beneath the first wave crest on the lee side
At approximately mountain peak height or below
Closed vortex: surface flow reverses toward mountain
Most hazardous region of the mountain wave system
Severe to extreme turbulence within the rotor

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: position and type of any possible cloud development.
concept: Lenticular (lens-shaped) clouds form at wave crests where air rises to the dew point — they are stationary. Rotor cloud (ragged cumulus) develops in the turbulent rotor zone under the first crest. Cap cloud may cover the mountain peak. If air is dry, no cloud forms but the wave still exists — a hazard not visible to the pilot.
exam_tip: Lenticular clouds at wave crests (stationary). Rotor cloud in rotor zone. Cap cloud on peak. No cloud ≠ no wave.
key_facts: Lenticular clouds at wave crests — stationary
Rotor cloud (ragged cumulus) in rotor zone beneath first crest
Cap cloud on mountain peak
Clouds stationary — air flows rapidly through them
No cloud if air is dry — wave still present

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: the heights of the friction layer.
concept: In mountain wave conditions, the normal friction layer is disrupted. The rotor zone creates deep turbulent mixing. Above the rotor, smooth laminar wave flow may exist with little friction. Large variations in wind speed/direction with height are characteristic of a wave system.
exam_tip: Mountain waves disrupt the normal friction layer. Rotor = deep turbulent mixing. Above rotor = laminar flow.
key_facts: Normal surface friction layer disrupted by wave activity
Rotor zone = deep turbulent mixing layer
Above the rotor: laminar wave flow, minimal friction
Large wind speed and direction variation with height

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: areas of probable severe turbulence.
concept: Severe turbulence is most probable: (1) in the rotor zone beneath the first wave crest; (2) on the immediate lee slope of the mountain in breaking wave zones; (3) at wave crest transitions with strong vertical wind shear. Mountain wave turbulence can extend to FL300+ as CAT.
exam_tip: Worst turbulence: rotor zone. Also: immediate lee slope, wave crest transitions. Can reach FL300+.
key_facts: Rotor zone = most severe turbulence area
Immediate lee slope near the mountain
Wave crest transitions with strong wind shear
Breaking wave zones
Turbulence can extend to FL300+ as CAT

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: areas of possible severe airframe icing.
concept: Severe icing occurs where visible moisture is present at 0°C to −20°C: in cap cloud (windward side), lenticular clouds at wave crests, and rotor clouds (which can contain large supercooled droplets). Descending air on the immediate lee side warms adiabatically, reducing icing risk there, but the rotor zone can have severe icing.
exam_tip: Icing in cap cloud, lenticular clouds, rotor clouds. Lee-side descent = adiabatic warming = less icing. Rotor clouds: severe icing possible.
key_facts: Cap cloud (windward): icing risk
Lenticular clouds at wave crests: icing risk
Rotor clouds: severe icing (large supercooled droplets)
Temperature 0°C to −20°C = maximum icing zone
Lee-side descending air warms → reduced icing there

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: With reference to mountain waves: explain the factors that affect the wave amplitude.
concept: Wave amplitude (vertical displacement) increases with: (1) Mountain height — higher barriers produce greater lifting. (2) Wind speed — stronger winds produce greater amplitude up to a wave-breaking threshold. (3) Wind direction — perpendicular to the ridge maximises amplitude. (4) Stability configuration — a stable layer just above the mountain with less stability below enhances amplitude. (5) Vertical wind shear — moderate shear favours wave development.
exam_tip: Greater amplitude: higher mountain, stronger wind perpendicular to ridge, stable layer above. Amplitude = vertical up/down extent of the wave.
key_facts: Mountain height: higher = greater amplitude
Wind speed: stronger (perpendicular to ridge) = greater amplitude
Wind direction: perpendicular to ridge = maximum amplitude
Stability: stable layer above mountain enhances wave
Vertical wind shear: moderate shear aids development

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: With reference to mountain waves: explain the factors that affect the wave-length.
concept: Wave-length increases with: (1) Wind speed — faster flow produces longer waves (λ ≈ 2πU/N). (2) Atmospheric stability — more stable air (higher N, shorter period) reduces wave-length; weaker stability increases it. Note: higher wind speed has the dominant effect in practice. Typical wave-lengths range 5–35 km.
exam_tip: Longer wave-length: stronger wind, more stable atmosphere. Formula: λ ≈ 2πU/N (U = wind speed, N = Brunt-Väisälä frequency).
key_facts: Wave-length increases with wind speed
Formula: λ ≈ 2πU/N
N = Brunt-Väisälä frequency (stability measure)
Typical wave-lengths: 5–35 km
Dominant factor in practice: wind speed

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: With reference to mountain waves: describe the flight conditions associated with mountain waves.
concept: In smooth wave flow (above the rotor): strong sustained updrafts and downdrafts often exceeding 2,000 ft/min, difficulty maintaining altitude, altimeter unreliable due to standing pressure anomalies, airspeed fluctuations. In rotor zones: severe to extreme turbulence, sudden large airspeed/altitude/attitude changes. On the lee slope: powerful downdrafts may prevent the aircraft climbing clear of terrain even at full power.
exam_tip: Above rotor: strong updrafts/downdrafts >2,000 ft/min, smooth but extreme. Rotor: severe-extreme turbulence. Lee slope: powerful downdrafts.
key_facts: Updrafts and downdrafts >2,000 ft/min in smooth wave
Altitude maintenance very difficult
Altimeter may be unreliable (pressure anomalies)
Rotor zone: severe to extreme turbulence
Lee slope: powerful downdrafts, may prevent terrain clearance
Airspeed fluctuations throughout

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Explain the rotor streaming process.
concept: Rotor streaming is the turbulent closed-circulation vortex that forms beneath the first wave crest on the lee side of a mountain. As the main airflow descends the lee slope then curves up into the first wave crest, flow separation occurs at low level. A closed vortex — the rotor — forms: surface air flows back toward the mountain, rises on the upwind edge, and re-enters the main flow at the wave crest level. The rotor extends from the surface to approximately the mountain peak height and persists as long as the wave is active.
exam_tip: Rotor = closed vortex beneath first wave crest. Surface flow reverses toward mountain. Rises on upwind side. Extends surface to ~peak height.
key_facts: Closed circulation beneath the first wave crest
Surface flow reversed — toward the mountain (upwind)
Air rises on upwind edge of rotor, sinks on lee edge
Extends from surface to approximately mountain peak height
Sustained by the wave structure above it
Most intense turbulence area in the wave system

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the flight conditions associated with rotor streaming.
concept: The rotor zone presents the most dangerous flight conditions in a mountain wave system: extreme turbulence (severe to extreme ICAO classification), sudden large airspeed changes (50+ kts possible), large altitude and attitude upsets, reversed surface winds causing unexpected crosswind/tailwind conditions, possible structural damage to the airframe, heavy icing in rotor cloud, and poor visibility. Aircraft must avoid rotor zones.
exam_tip: Rotor = extreme turbulence, 50+ kt airspeed changes, reversed surface wind, structural damage risk. Heavy icing in rotor cloud. AVOID.
key_facts: Severe to extreme turbulence
Sudden airspeed changes 50+ knots
Large attitude and altitude upsets
Reversed surface winds below the rotor
Heavy icing possible in rotor cloud
Risk of structural damage
MUST AVOID — do not penetrate deliberately

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Define the Föhn wind.
concept: A Föhn wind is a warm, dry wind that descends on the lee side of a mountain range. Moist air rises on the windward side cooling at the SALR (~6°C/1,000 m) while precipitating; it then descends on the lee side warming at the faster DALR (~10°C/1,000 m). Because DALR > SALR, the air arrives at lee-side base level warmer and drier than it was at the same altitude on the windward side. In New Zealand the Föhn wind is known as the nor'wester, affecting the eastern South Island (Canterbury) after crossing the Southern Alps.
exam_tip: Föhn = warm dry wind on lee side. Rises at SALR (with rain), descends at DALR (dry). DALR > SALR → arrives warmer. NZ: nor'wester.
key_facts: Warm, dry wind on the lee side of a mountain range
Windward ascent at SALR (~6°C/1,000 m) — precipitation occurs
Lee descent at DALR (~10°C/1,000 m) — dry air warms faster
Net effect: air is warmer and drier than same altitude windward
Key mechanism: DALR (10°C/1,000 m) > SALR (6°C/1,000 m)
New Zealand example: nor'wester over Canterbury

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the development of a Föhn wind.
concept: Requirements: (1) Substantial mountain barrier to force air to significant altitude. (2) Moist air on the windward side — must condense and precipitate during ascent (without precipitation the temperature gain does not occur). (3) Sufficient cross-barrier pressure gradient to force air over the range. In NZ, the nor'wester requires a northwest pressure gradient pushing moist Tasman Sea air up over the Southern Alps.
exam_tip: Föhn needs: (1) large mountain barrier, (2) moist windward air (must rain!), (3) adequate cross-mountain pressure gradient. No rain = no Föhn effect.
key_facts: Substantial mountain range required
Moist air on windward side — condensation and precipitation must occur
Sufficient pressure gradient to force air over the range
No precipitation on windward side = no temperature gain (dry Föhn)
NZ: requires NW pressure gradient and moist Tasman Sea air

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the flight conditions when flying in Föhn conditions in the following positions: to windward of the mountain range.
concept: On the windward side: strong orographic lifting, cloud build-up on slopes, heavy rain or snow, turbulence in updraft zones, low ceilings, reduced visibility in precipitation, and significant icing risk in cloud above the freezing level. Weather is deteriorating and flying conditions are challenging.
exam_tip: Windward side: orographic lifting, cloud, heavy precipitation, turbulence, low ceilings, icing risk. Conditions deteriorating.
key_facts: Forced orographic lifting
Cloud development on windward slopes
Heavy rain or snow
Turbulence in updraft zones
Low ceilings and reduced visibility
Icing risk in cloud above freezing level

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the flight conditions when flying in Föhn conditions in the following positions: over the mountain range.
concept: Over the range: severe turbulence, updrafts and downdrafts often exceeding 2,000 ft/min, mountain waves likely to be active (wave/rotor system), cap cloud reducing visibility over peaks, serious icing in cloud. The combination of severe turbulence and reduced visibility makes this the most dangerous zone.
exam_tip: Over the range: severe turbulence, >2,000 ft/min vertical drafts, waves/rotors active, cap cloud, icing. Most dangerous zone.
key_facts: Severe turbulence over the ridge
Updrafts and downdrafts >2,000 ft/min
Mountain waves commonly present (wave/rotor system)
Cap cloud reduces visibility over peaks
Icing in cloud
Most dangerous zone for flight in Föhn conditions

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the flight conditions when flying in Föhn conditions in the following positions: on the lee side of the mountain range.
concept: On the lee side: warm dry descending air, strong gusty and variable surface winds, rotor turbulence close to the mountain base, strong downdrafts near the range (may prevent terrain clearance at full power). Farther from the mountains: warming temperatures, improving visibility, clearing cloud. Föhn wall cloud marks the boundary between dry warm air and any residual cloud.
exam_tip: Lee side: warm dry air, gusty variable winds, rotor turbulence near base, strong downdrafts close to range. Further away: warm, dry, clearing.
key_facts: Warm, dry descending air
Strong gusty variable surface winds
Rotor turbulence at low level near mountain base
Strong downdrafts close to the range
Föhn wall cloud marks boundary of warm dry air
Farther from range: warming, improving visibility, clearing cloud

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the development of sea breezes about the islands of the sub-tropical SW Pacific.
concept: During the day, land heats more rapidly than the surrounding ocean (land has lower specific heat capacity). Warmer air over the land rises, creating a surface low over land. Cooler denser sea air flows in at the surface — the sea breeze. The sea breeze front moves inland through the day. On small tropical/subtropical islands, sea breezes converge from all sides, triggering intense afternoon convective showers and thunderstorms at the convergence zone over the island interior.
exam_tip: Sea breeze: land heats faster → rising air → sea air flows in at surface. On small islands: convergence from all sides → afternoon thunderstorms.
key_facts: Land heats faster than sea (lower specific heat capacity)
Warm rising air over land creates surface low
Cool dense sea air flows in = sea breeze
Sea breeze front moves inland during the day
Night: land cools faster → land breeze (reversed)
Small islands: sea breeze convergence → afternoon convective thunderstorms

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the south-east trade winds about the islands of the sub-tropical SW Pacific.
concept: The SE trade winds are the dominant low-level winds over the sub-tropical SW Pacific, driven by the pressure gradient between the subtropical high (~25–30°S) and the equatorial low. They are consistent in direction (SE, Coriolis-deflected) and speed (10–25 kts), present year-round. They produce markedly different weather on windward (SE) versus leeward (NW) sides of islands — heavy orographic rain on the SE windward side, dry conditions on the NW leeward side. A trade wind inversion at ~1,500–2,000 m caps convective cloud development.
exam_tip: SE trade winds: consistent SE 10–25 kt. Windward SE = wet/cloudy. Leeward NW = dry. Trade wind inversion at ~1,500–2,000 m caps cloud.
key_facts: Driven by pressure gradient: subtropical high → equatorial low
SE direction (Coriolis deflected) in Southern Hemisphere
Consistent speed: ~10–25 kts, year-round
Windward (SE) side: heavy orographic rain, cloud
Leeward (NW) side: dry, sunny conditions
Trade wind inversion at ~1,500–2,000 m caps convective cloud

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe: a katabatic wind.
concept: A katabatic wind is a downslope drainage wind caused by radiative cooling of elevated terrain. On clear calm nights, the elevated surface (plateau, glacier, hillside) radiates heat to space, cooling the overlying air. The cooled air becomes denser than surrounding air and flows downslope under gravity. Katabatic winds are strongest on clear, calm nights and can cause frost hollows in New Zealand valleys.
exam_tip: Katabatic: cold dense air drains downslope under gravity (radiative cooling of elevated terrain). Strongest clear calm nights. Frost hollows in NZ valleys.
key_facts: Cold dense air flows downslope under gravity
Caused by radiative cooling of elevated terrain
Strongest on clear, calm nights
NZ: common in mountain valleys, causes frost hollows
Can be severe on steep slopes and glaciers
Katabatic = downslope flow

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe: an anabatic wind.
concept: An anabatic wind is an upslope wind caused by solar heating of a hillside. The sun warms the slope surface, heating the overlying air. This air becomes warmer and lighter than air at the same altitude over the valley, and rises up the slope. Anabatic winds are a daytime phenomenon and typically light (5–15 kts). They can trigger cumulus development on ridges and hilltops in the afternoon.
exam_tip: Anabatic: warm air rises upslope due to daytime solar heating of hillside. Triggers afternoon cumulus. Anabatic = upslope.
key_facts: Warm air rises upslope due to solar heating
Daytime phenomenon — solar driven
Typically light: 5–15 kts
Triggers afternoon cumulus on ridges and hilltops
Opposite of katabatic
Anabatic = upslope flow

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions that support the development of: katabatic winds.
concept: Conditions favouring katabatic winds: clear skies (maximum long-wave radiation cooling); light synoptic winds (strong winds destroy the drainage flow); high elevation starting terrain (plateau, glacier, steep slopes); night-time or post-sunset; low humidity (dry air allows maximum radiative cooling); calm synoptic conditions (no strong pressure gradient to disrupt local drainage).
exam_tip: Katabatic conditions: clear skies + light winds + high elevation terrain + night-time + dry air.
key_facts: Clear skies: maximum radiative cooling
Light synoptic winds: strong winds destroy drainage flow
High elevation terrain: plateaus, glaciers, steep slopes
Night-time or post-sunset
Low humidity: dry air cools more effectively
Calm synoptic conditions

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions that support the development of: anabatic winds.
concept: Conditions favouring anabatic winds: strong solar heating (clear skies, long daylight); steep slopes well-exposed to the sun (maximises insolation on slope surface); light synoptic winds (strong winds disrupt the anabatic circulation); daytime conditions only.
exam_tip: Anabatic conditions: clear sky + strong sunshine + steep sun-facing slopes + light synoptic winds + daytime.
key_facts: Strong solar heating: clear skies
Steep, well-exposed, sun-facing slopes
Light synoptic winds (strong winds disrupt circulation)
Daytime phenomenon only

syllabus_desc: Explain the sources of water vapour in the atmosphere.
concept: Water vapour enters the atmosphere primarily through evaporation from ocean surfaces (the dominant source, ~85% of total), lakes, rivers, and moist soil. Transpiration from vegetation is also a significant source. Evaporation rate is driven by temperature (warm water evaporates faster), wind speed (removes saturated air from the surface), and the vapour pressure deficit (difference between saturation VP and actual VP). Most atmospheric water vapour is concentrated in the lowest 2 km of the atmosphere because that is where the sources are and where temperatures are highest.
exam_tip: Main sources: evaporation (oceans ~85%), lakes, moist soil, transpiration from vegetation. Most water vapour is in the lowest 2 km.
key_facts: Evaporation from oceans: dominant source (~85% of total)
Evaporation from lakes, rivers, moist soil
Transpiration from vegetation
Driven by: temperature, wind speed, vapour pressure deficit
Most water vapour concentrated in lowest 2 km of atmosphere
Water vapour decreases rapidly with altitude

syllabus_desc: Describe 'vapour pressure' and 'saturation vapour pressure'.
concept: Vapour pressure is the partial pressure exerted by water vapour in the atmosphere — it is the contribution of water molecules to total atmospheric pressure. Saturation vapour pressure (SVP) is the maximum vapour pressure possible at a given temperature — the point at which the air is holding as much water vapour as it can. SVP increases rapidly with temperature (roughly doubling for every 10°C rise). When actual vapour pressure equals SVP, the air is saturated (relative humidity = 100%) and further cooling will cause condensation. The dew point is the temperature at which SVP equals the actual vapour pressure.
exam_tip: Vapour pressure = partial pressure of water vapour. Saturation VP = maximum at a given temperature. SVP doubles every ~10°C. At saturation: RH = 100%.
key_facts: Vapour pressure = partial pressure of water vapour in air
Saturation vapour pressure (SVP) = maximum possible at a given temperature
SVP increases rapidly with temperature (roughly doubles per 10°C)
RH = 100% when actual VP equals SVP
Dew point = temperature at which air becomes saturated
Cooling below dew point → condensation

syllabus_desc: Define 'latent heat'.
concept: Latent heat is the heat energy absorbed or released during a phase change (change of state) of water, without any change in temperature. When water evaporates, it absorbs latent heat from the surroundings (cooling effect — this is the latent heat of vaporisation, ~2,500 J/g). When water vapour condenses, it releases this latent heat, warming the surrounding air. When water freezes, it releases latent heat of fusion (~334 J/g). When ice sublimates directly to vapour, it absorbs both latent heat of fusion and vaporisation. Latent heat release in clouds drives convection and is the energy source for severe weather.
exam_tip: Latent heat = heat absorbed/released during phase change (no temperature change). Evaporation absorbs heat; condensation releases heat. Drives convective weather.
key_facts: Heat absorbed or released during phase change (no temperature change)
Evaporation: absorbs latent heat (~2,500 J/g) — cools surroundings
Condensation: releases latent heat — warms surrounding air
Freezing: releases latent heat of fusion (~334 J/g)
Sublimation: absorbs both fusion and vaporisation latent heat
Latent heat release in clouds is the energy source for severe weather

syllabus_desc: Describe the condensation process.
concept: Condensation occurs when moist air is cooled below its dew point, causing water vapour to change state to liquid water. Condensation requires condensation nuclei — tiny particles (dust, sea salt, pollutants, combustion products) onto which water molecules can attach. Without nuclei, air can become supersaturated. The condensation process releases latent heat, which partially counteracts the cooling and explains why the Saturated Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR) is less steep than the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR). Condensation produces cloud, fog, dew, and precipitation.
exam_tip: Condensation: cooling below dew point + condensation nuclei → water vapour to liquid. Releases latent heat → SALR < DALR.
key_facts: Cooling below dew point triggers condensation
Condensation nuclei required (dust, salt, pollutants)
Releases latent heat — partially offsets cooling
Explains SALR < DALR (latent heat release slows cooling)
Produces: cloud, fog, dew, precipitation

syllabus_desc: Describe the freezing and melting processes with reference to latent heat.
concept: When liquid water freezes to ice, latent heat of fusion (~334 J/g) is released, warming the surrounding air. When ice melts, it absorbs the same amount of latent heat, cooling the surrounding air. These latent heat exchanges have important aviation implications: melting snow and ice on the runway can create a temporary cooling effect; the 0°C level in cloud is not always a sharp boundary due to latent heat exchanges; freezing rain occurs when super-cooled rain droplets freeze on contact with surfaces below 0°C, releasing latent heat briefly before the structure cools.
exam_tip: Freezing: releases latent heat (~334 J/g). Melting: absorbs latent heat. Exchanges important at 0°C level in cloud and on surfaces.
key_facts: Freezing releases latent heat of fusion (~334 J/g)
Melting absorbs latent heat — cools surrounding air
0°C level in cloud not sharp due to latent heat exchanges
Freezing rain: super-cooled droplets freeze on contact with cold surfaces
Latent heat exchanges affect temperature at and below the freezing level

syllabus_desc: Describe the diurnal variation of relative humidity and dew point.
concept: Dew point (the actual moisture content of the air) remains relatively constant throughout the day unless moist advection occurs or precipitation falls. Relative humidity (RH), however, varies significantly with temperature: it peaks at night/early morning (temperature is lowest, closer to dew point) and is lowest in the afternoon (temperature highest, further from dew point). The diurnal range of RH can be 30–50% or more in continental regions. This explains why radiation fog typically forms at night/dawn (when RH reaches 100%) and dissipates after sunrise.
exam_tip: Dew point: relatively constant daily. RH: highest at night (RH = 100% → fog), lowest in afternoon. RH varies inversely with temperature.
key_facts: Dew point is relatively constant throughout the day
Relative humidity varies inversely with temperature
RH peaks at night/early morning when temperature is lowest
RH is lowest in the afternoon when temperature is highest
Radiation fog forms when night-time RH reaches 100%
Fog typically dissipates after sunrise as temperature rises

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of moisture content on the density of the air.
concept: Moist air is less dense than dry air at the same temperature and pressure. This seems counterintuitive but is because water vapour (molecular weight 18) is lighter than nitrogen (28) or oxygen (32) — adding water vapour displaces heavier molecules. Therefore, higher humidity → lower air density. This directly affects aircraft performance: high humidity reduces density altitude performance in the same way as high temperature. In performance calculations, density altitude increases slightly with humidity. The effect is relatively small compared to temperature but is significant at high temperatures and high humidity combinations.
exam_tip: Moist air is LESS dense than dry air. Water vapour (mol wt 18) displaces heavier N₂ (28) and O₂ (32). High humidity → lower density → worse performance.
key_facts: Moist air is less dense than dry air (at same temp and pressure)
Water vapour (mol wt 18) lighter than N₂ (28) and O₂ (32)
Displacing heavier molecules → overall lower density
High humidity → lower density altitude performance
Effect is additive with high temperature effects
Density altitude increases with humidity

syllabus_desc: Explain how atmospheric stability is determined.
concept: Atmospheric stability is determined by comparing the Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR — actual temperature change with altitude) with the appropriate adiabatic lapse rate. For unsaturated (dry) air: compare ELR with DALR (10°C/1,000 m). For saturated (moist) air: compare ELR with SALR (~6°C/1,000 m). If ELR > DALR (or SALR): UNSTABLE — a lifted parcel continues to rise. If ELR < DALR (and SALR): STABLE — a lifted parcel returns to original level. If ELR = DALR (or SALR): NEUTRAL — a lifted parcel neither rises nor sinks.
exam_tip: Stability: compare ELR with DALR (dry) or SALR (saturated). ELR > DALR = unstable. ELR < SALR = stable. Between SALR and DALR = conditionally unstable.
key_facts: ELR = Environmental (actual) Lapse Rate
DALR = 10°C/1,000 m (dry adiabatic)
SALR ≈ 6°C/1,000 m (saturated adiabatic)
ELR > DALR → absolutely unstable (parcel keeps rising)
ELR < SALR → absolutely stable (parcel returns to origin)
SALR < ELR < DALR → conditionally unstable (unstable if saturated)
ELR = DALR or SALR → neutral stability

syllabus_desc: Given plotted graphs of temperature (ELR) versus height, identify and describe: super-adiabatic temperature lapse-rates (steep ELRs).
concept: A super-adiabatic lapse rate is where the ELR is steeper than the DALR (ELR > 10°C/1,000 m). On a Tephigram or temperature-height graph, this appears as a line steeper than the dry adiabat. Super-adiabatic conditions are absolutely unstable — a parcel lifted even slightly will be warmer and less dense than its environment and will accelerate upward. This creates vigorous convection and thermals. It typically occurs in the lowest few hundred feet on sunny afternoons when the surface is strongly heated.
exam_tip: Super-adiabatic: ELR > DALR (>10°C/1,000 m). Steeper than dry adiabat on graph. Absolutely unstable. Vigorous thermals. Surface heating.
key_facts: Super-adiabatic = ELR > DALR (>10°C/1,000 m)
Absolutely unstable — lifted parcel is always warmer than environment
Parcel accelerates upward without limit
Vigorous convection and thermals
Occurs on sunny afternoons in lowest few hundred feet
Appears as steeper-than-dry-adiabat line on temp-height graph

syllabus_desc: Given plotted graphs of temperature (ELR) versus height, identify and describe: inversions and isothermal layers (shallow ELRs).
concept: An inversion is where temperature increases with altitude (ELR is negative — the line on a temp-height graph slopes backward). An isothermal layer has constant temperature with altitude (vertical line on graph). Both are absolutely stable conditions — a lifted parcel will be much cooler than its environment and will sink back. Inversions trap pollutants, smoke, and moisture below the inversion base, and cap convective cloud development. Common types: radiation inversion (surface cooling), subsidence inversion (descending air in highs), frontal inversion.
exam_tip: Inversion: temperature increases with altitude (ELR negative). Isothermal: constant temperature. Both absolutely stable. Cap cloud development, trap pollution.
key_facts: Inversion: temperature increases with altitude
Isothermal: constant temperature with altitude
Both are absolutely stable
Lifted parcel is much cooler than environment → sinks back
Inversions cap convective cloud development
Trap pollutants, smoke, and moisture below
Types: radiation, subsidence, frontal inversions

syllabus_desc: Calculate atmospheric stability by lifting parcels of air given assumed ELR's, dew point temperatures and mountain heights.
concept: To calculate stability over a mountain: (1) Start with surface temperature and dew point. (2) Lift the parcel dry-adiabatically at DALR (−10°C/1,000 m) until it reaches the Lifting Condensation Level (LCL — where it cools to dew point). (3) Continue lifting at SALR (−6°C/1,000 m) above the LCL. (4) Compare parcel temperature to the ELR at each height. If parcel is warmer than environment: unstable/convective. If cooler: stable. For mountain crossing: check if parcel remains stable or triggers convection, and what temperature the parcel will be on the lee side (Föhn effect if precipitation has occurred).
exam_tip: Stability calc: lift dry at DALR to LCL, then at SALR. Compare parcel temp to ELR. Parcel warmer = unstable. Also calculates Föhn effect on lee side.
key_facts: Lift unsaturated parcel at DALR (−10°C/1,000 m)
LCL = altitude where parcel temperature = dew point (start of cloud)
Above LCL: lift at SALR (~−6°C/1,000 m)
Compare parcel temperature to ELR at each level
Parcel warmer than ELR = unstable (continues to rise)
Parcel cooler than ELR = stable (returns to original level)
Föhn effect: lee-side temperature calculated from DALR descent

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical diurnal variation of stability.
concept: Stability follows a predictable daily cycle driven by solar heating. At night: surface cools by radiation → surface-based inversion forms → atmosphere very stable → fog/stratus possible. Around dawn: most stable, inversion deepest. After sunrise: solar heating warms the surface → inversion erodes from below → unstable layer develops. Mid-afternoon: most unstable (peak instability), cumulus development maximum, thermals strongest. After sunset: surface cooling begins, instability decreases, atmosphere stabilises. The pattern repeats daily in the absence of synoptic-scale disturbances.
exam_tip: Night → stable (radiation inversion). Dawn → most stable. After sunrise → inversion erodes, instability builds. Afternoon → most unstable (peak thermals). After sunset → stabilises.
key_facts: Night: surface cools → radiation inversion forms → stable
Dawn: most stable conditions of the day
After sunrise: solar heating erodes inversion from below
Mid-afternoon: most unstable — peak thermals and cumulus
After sunset: stability increases as surface cools
Cycle repeats daily under settled conditions

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between stability of air and cloud type.
concept: Atmospheric stability determines cloud type. Unstable air: vigorous convection produces cumuliform clouds (cumulus, cumulonimbus) — vertical development, towering, associated with showers and thunderstorms. Stable air: gradual lifting or gentle ascent produces stratiform clouds (stratus, stratocumulus, altostratus) — horizontal layers, little vertical extent, associated with steady drizzle or rain. Conditional instability (between DALR and SALR): initially stable but becomes unstable after reaching the LCL — produces large cumulus and Cb if the LCL is reached.
exam_tip: Unstable = cumuliform clouds (vertical, showery). Stable = stratiform (layered, steady rain). Conditional instability = large cumulus/Cb once LCL is reached.
key_facts: Unstable air → cumuliform clouds (Cu, Cb)
Stable air → stratiform clouds (St, Sc, As)
Cumuliform: vigorous vertical development, showers, thunderstorms
Stratiform: horizontal layers, steady drizzle or continuous rain
Conditional instability → large cumulus/Cb if LCL is reached
Cloud type is a direct indicator of atmospheric stability

syllabus_desc: List the vertical extents of the three main cloud layers in: mid-latitudes.
concept: In mid-latitudes (approx 30–60°S/N), the three cloud layers are: Low cloud — surface to 6,500 ft (2,000 m). Medium cloud — 6,500 ft to 23,000 ft (2,000–7,000 m). High cloud — 16,500 ft to 43,000 ft (5,000–13,000 m). Note: the layers overlap because the tropopause is lower at higher latitudes.
exam_tip: Mid-latitudes cloud layers: Low = surface–6,500 ft; Medium = 6,500–23,000 ft; High = 16,500–43,000 ft.
key_facts: Low cloud: surface to 6,500 ft (2,000 m)
Medium cloud: 6,500 ft to 23,000 ft (2,000–7,000 m)
Applies in mid-latitudes (~30–60°)
Layers overlap — no sharp boundaries

syllabus_desc: List the vertical extents of the three main cloud layers in: tropical latitudes.
concept: In tropical latitudes (approx 0–30°S/N), with a higher tropopause, cloud layers extend higher: Low cloud — surface to 6,500 ft (2,000 m). Medium cloud — 6,500 ft to 26,000 ft (2,000–8,000 m). High cloud — 20,000 ft to 60,000 ft (6,000–18,000 m).
exam_tip: Tropical cloud layers: Low = surface–6,500 ft; Medium = 6,500–26,000 ft; High = 20,000–60,000 ft. Higher than mid-latitudes.
key_facts: Low cloud: surface to 6,500 ft (2,000 m)
Medium cloud: 6,500 ft to 26,000 ft (2,000–8,000 m)
Higher tropopause in tropics → clouds extend higher
Applies in tropical latitudes (~0–30°)

syllabus_desc: State the difference between the Lifting Condensation Level (LCL) and the Convective Condensation Level (CCL).
concept: The LCL (Lifting Condensation Level) is the altitude at which a parcel of air, lifted mechanically (e.g. by orographic or frontal forcing), cools to its dew point and condensation begins. It determines cloud base for forced-lifting clouds (stratus, orographic cloud). The CCL (Convective Condensation Level) is the altitude at which a parcel, heated from the surface and rising convectively, becomes saturated. It determines the base of convective (cumuliform) clouds. The CCL is generally higher than the LCL because surface heating raises the parcel temperature, requiring more cooling before saturation is reached. In very humid, unstable conditions the LCL and CCL may be close together.
exam_tip: LCL = cloud base for mechanically lifted air. CCL = cloud base for convectively rising air. CCL usually HIGHER than LCL. LCL = forced lifting; CCL = surface heating.
key_facts: LCL = Lifting Condensation Level: base of forced-lifting clouds
CCL = Convective Condensation Level: base of convective (Cu) clouds
LCL: parcel lifted mechanically to saturation
CCL: parcel heated from surface, rises convectively to saturation
CCL is generally higher than LCL
LCL determines cloud base for frontal/orographic cloud
CCL determines cumulus cloud base on convective days

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate the use of simple formulae to calculate the LCL and CCL.
concept: LCL calculation: LCL height (ft) ≈ (Surface temperature − Dew point) × 400 [using °C and feet]. This is because the DALR is 3°C/1,000 ft and the dew point lapse rate is approximately 0.5°C/1,000 ft, giving a net temperature–dew point convergence of ~2.5°C/1,000 ft, or ~400 ft/°C spread. CCL calculation: CCL height (ft) ≈ (Surface temperature − Dew point) × 250 [approximation for convective conditions where surface heating raises temperature]. Example: Temp 25°C, Dew point 15°C → spread = 10°C → LCL ≈ 4,000 ft; CCL ≈ 2,500 ft.
exam_tip: LCL height ≈ (Temp − Dew point) × 400 ft/°C. CCL height ≈ (Temp − Dew point) × 250 ft/°C. Example: 10°C spread → LCL ≈ 4,000 ft.
key_facts: LCL formula: (Temp − Dew point) × 400 = height in feet
CCL formula: (Temp − Dew point) × 250 = height in feet (approx)
Based on DALR 3°C/1,000 ft and dew point lapse 0.5°C/1,000 ft
Net convergence rate ~2.5°C/1,000 ft → 400 ft per °C spread
Example: 10°C spread → LCL ≈ 4,000 ft AGL
These are approximations — actual cloud base varies

syllabus_desc: Describe the 10 main cloud types as defined by the WMO.
concept: The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) defines 10 cloud genera: HIGH: Cirrus (Ci) — thin ice crystal wisps; Cirrocumulus (Cc) — small white puffs in rows; Cirrostratus (Cs) — thin ice sheet producing halos. MEDIUM: Altocumulus (Ac) — grey/white patches, larger than Cc; Altostratus (As) — grey/blue sheet, sun through ground glass. LOW: Stratus (St) — low grey layer, drizzle; Stratocumulus (Sc) — grey/white rolls or patches, most common cloud. VERTICAL: Nimbostratus (Ns) — dark rain cloud; Cumulus (Cu) — white puffy, flat base, cauliflower top; Cumulonimbus (Cb) — tall storm cloud, anvil top, thunderstorm.
exam_tip: 10 WMO cloud genera: High: Ci, Cc, Cs. Medium: Ac, As. Low: St, Sc. Vertical: Ns, Cu, Cb. Cb = thunderstorm cloud.
key_facts: HIGH clouds (ice): Cirrus (Ci), Cirrocumulus (Cc), Cirrostratus (Cs)
MEDIUM clouds: Altocumulus (Ac), Altostratus (As)
LOW clouds: Stratus (St), Stratocumulus (Sc)
VERTICAL: Nimbostratus (Ns), Cumulus (Cu), Cumulonimbus (Cb)
Cirrostratus: halo around sun/moon (ice crystals)
Altostratus: sun visible through ground glass
Cumulonimbus: thunderstorm cloud with anvil top
Stratocumulus: most common cloud type

syllabus_desc: Describe typical conditions for each of the 10 main cloud types with respect to: turbulence.
concept: Turbulence by cloud type: Cumulonimbus (Cb): extreme turbulence inside and nearby. Cumulus (Cu): light to moderate turbulence in and below cloud; severe in large Cu/TCu. Nimbostratus (Ns): light turbulence generally. Altostratus (As): light turbulence. Altocumulus (Ac): light to moderate, especially Ac Cas. Stratocumulus (Sc): light turbulence. Stratus (St): smooth. Cirrostratus/Cirrus/Cirrocumulus: smooth to light CAT possible near Ci associated with jet streams.
exam_tip: Turbulence: Cb = extreme. TCu/large Cu = severe. Ac Cas = moderate. Ns/Sc = light. Ci/Cs = smooth (CAT possible near jets). St = smooth.
key_facts: Cumulonimbus: extreme turbulence (avoid by 20 nm)
Towering Cumulus (TCu): severe turbulence
Large Cumulus: moderate to severe turbulence
Altocumulus Castellanus: moderate turbulence
Nimbostratus: light turbulence
Stratocumulus: light turbulence
Stratus: smooth
High clouds: generally smooth (CAT possible near jet streams)

syllabus_desc: Describe typical conditions for each of the 10 main cloud types with respect to: icing.
concept: Icing by cloud type: Cumulonimbus: severe icing (large supercooled droplets). Nimbostratus/Altostratus: moderate to severe icing. Stratocumulus/Stratus: moderate icing, especially in winter. Altocumulus: light to moderate icing. Cumulus: moderate icing above freezing level. Cirrus/Cirrostratus/Cirrocumulus: ice crystals only — no significant accretion on aircraft (ice crystal icing is now recognised but less severe than supercooled water icing). Highest icing risk: between 0°C and −20°C in clouds containing supercooled water droplets.
exam_tip: Worst icing: Cb, Ns, As (supercooled water). High clouds (Ci, Cs): ice crystals only — low accretion risk. Peak icing zone: 0°C to −20°C.
key_facts: Cumulonimbus: severe icing (large supercooled droplets)
Nimbostratus and Altostratus: moderate to severe icing
Stratocumulus/Stratus: moderate icing especially in winter
Altocumulus: light to moderate icing
Cirrus/Cirrostratus/Cirrocumulus: ice crystals, low accretion
Peak icing zone: 0°C to −20°C
Supercooled water droplets cause airframe ice accretion

syllabus_desc: Describe typical conditions for each of the 10 main cloud types with respect to: precipitation.
concept: Precipitation by cloud type: Nimbostratus: continuous rain or snow. Cumulonimbus: heavy showers, hail, possibly tornadoes. Stratus: drizzle (fine drops). Altostratus: light rain or snow. Stratocumulus: light rain or drizzle. Altocumulus: virga (precipitation evaporating before reaching ground). Cumulus: light showers from larger Cu. Cirrus/Cirrostratus: no precipitation reaching ground. Cirrocumulus: no significant precipitation.
exam_tip: Rain producers: Ns (steady), Cb (heavy showers/hail), St (drizzle), As (light rain). Ci/Cs/Cc: no surface precipitation. Virga from Ac.
key_facts: Nimbostratus: continuous rain or snow
Cumulonimbus: heavy showers, hail, possibly tornadoes
Stratus: drizzle
Altostratus: light rain or snow
Stratocumulus: light rain or drizzle
Altocumulus: virga (precipitation not reaching ground)
Cirrus/Cirrostratus/Cirrocumulus: no precipitation at surface

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Asperitas.
concept: Asperitas (classified by WMO in 2017) appears as a chaotic, wave-like undulating base on a layer cloud, resembling a rough sea surface viewed from below. It forms when turbulent mixing occurs below a cloud layer, creating irregular undulations in the cloud base. Asperitas indicates turbulent conditions below the cloud layer — moderate turbulence can be expected at and below cloud base. It is most commonly seen on stratocumulus or altocumulus layers and is associated with atmospheric instability near cloud base.
exam_tip: Asperitas: chaotic wave-like cloud base. Looks like rough sea from below. Indicates turbulence at/below cloud base. Forms in stratocumulus or altocumulus.
key_facts: Classified by WMO in 2017
Chaotic, wave-like undulating cloud base
Resembles rough sea surface viewed from below
Indicates turbulent conditions at and below cloud base
Associated with instability near cloud base
Seen on Sc or Ac layers

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Mammatus.
concept: Mammatus are rounded, pouch-like protrusions hanging from the underside of a cloud, typically the anvil of a cumulonimbus. They form due to downward convection of cloud particles — the opposite of normal cloud formation. Mammatus indicate severe atmospheric instability and the presence of a large, organised convective system. When seen on a Cb anvil, they indicate the cell is highly developed and very active. Extreme turbulence is likely in and near the cloud.
exam_tip: Mammatus: rounded pouches hanging from Cb anvil underside. Formed by downward convection. Indicates severe instability. Cb is highly developed — extreme turbulence.
key_facts: Rounded, pouch-like protrusions hanging beneath cloud
Typically seen on the underside of Cb anvil
Formed by downward convection (sinking saturated air)
Indicates severe atmospheric instability
Highly developed, very active Cb
Extreme turbulence likely in vicinity

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Altocumulus Lenticularis.
concept: Altocumulus Lenticularis (Ac Len) are lens-shaped, smooth-edged lenticular clouds that form in the crests of mountain waves at medium altitudes. They are stationary — air flows rapidly through them but the cloud remains in position over the wave crest. Their presence indicates mountain wave activity, with potentially strong turbulence in the rotor zone below and severe updrafts/downdrafts. They may also form at high altitudes (Cc Len) and low altitudes (Sc Len). Smooth appearance indicates laminar wave flow at their altitude.
exam_tip: Ac Len: smooth lens-shaped stationary cloud at medium altitude in mountain wave crests. Indicates wave activity — rotor/turbulence below. Smooth = laminar flow at cloud level.
key_facts: Lens-shaped (lenticular), smooth-edged cloud
Forms in mountain wave crests at medium altitudes
Stationary — air flows through it but cloud does not move
Indicates mountain wave activity
Rotor turbulence likely below
Strong updrafts/downdrafts within the wave
Smooth appearance = laminar wave flow at that level

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Rotor Cloud.
concept: Rotor cloud is a ragged, lumpy mass of cumulus-like cloud that forms in the turbulent rotor zone beneath the first mountain wave crest. Unlike lenticular clouds (which are smooth), rotor cloud is chaotic and agitated in appearance. It indicates the presence of a rotor — extreme turbulence, reversed surface winds, and large wind shear. Rotor cloud is the most dangerous cloud type for aviation in mountain wave situations. Flight through rotor cloud should be avoided.
exam_tip: Rotor cloud: ragged/lumpy Cu-like cloud beneath first wave crest. Unlike lenticular (smooth), rotor cloud = chaotic. Indicates extreme turbulence and reversed surface winds. AVOID.
key_facts: Ragged, lumpy cumulus-like cloud in the rotor zone
Beneath the first mountain wave crest on the lee side
Indicates extreme turbulence (rotor zone)
Reversed surface winds below the rotor
Chaotic in appearance unlike smooth lenticular clouds
Most dangerous aviation cloud in mountain wave conditions
MUST AVOID

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Kelvin Helmholtz waves.
concept: Kelvin Helmholtz (KH) waves appear as breaking wave shapes (like ocean waves about to break) in cloud layers. They form at the boundary between two air layers moving at different speeds — the shear at the interface creates wave instabilities that break. They indicate significant vertical wind shear between layers. From an aviation perspective, KH waves indicate moderate to severe turbulence in the shear zone. They are most commonly seen on the upper surface of stable cloud layers.
exam_tip: KH waves: breaking-wave shaped cloud indicating vertical wind shear between layers. Moderate to severe turbulence in the shear zone.
key_facts: Breaking wave shape in cloud layers
Forms at interface between two air layers of different speed
Indicates vertical wind shear
Moderate to severe turbulence in shear zone
Seen on upper surface of stable cloud layers
Named after Kelvin and Helmholtz fluid dynamics instability

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Altocumulus Castellanus.
concept: Altocumulus Castellanus (Ac Cas) appears as medium-level altocumulus with crenellated or castle-like turrets projecting upward from its base. It forms when a moist layer at mid-levels is overlain by conditional instability. Ac Cas is a reliable early warning indicator of afternoon convective development — it typically develops in the morning and indicates that deep convection (large cumulus, thunderstorms) is likely by the afternoon. Pilots should be cautious of convective weather development if Ac Cas is observed in the morning.
exam_tip: Ac Cas: altocumulus with castle-like turrets projecting upward. EARLY WARNING of afternoon thunderstorm development. If seen in the morning → expect Cb by afternoon.
key_facts: Altocumulus with crenellated (castle-like) turrets projecting upward
Indicates conditional instability at mid-levels
Early morning indicator of afternoon convective development
If Ac Cas seen in morning → thunderstorms likely that afternoon
Reliable pre-convective indicator
Light to moderate turbulence in the cloud itself

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Banner cloud.
concept: A banner cloud is a stationary cloud that streams from one side of a prominent mountain peak like a flag or banner, while the other side may be clear. It forms on the lee side where orographic lift and turbulent eddies cause moist air to rise and condense. The cloud appears to be attached to the peak and streams downwind. Banner clouds are common on isolated sharp peaks (e.g. the Matterhorn). They indicate strong orographic forcing, turbulence on the lee side of the peak, and possible icing in the cloud.
exam_tip: Banner cloud: stationary cloud streaming from leeward side of a mountain peak like a flag. Indicates orographic lifting, lee turbulence, icing risk.
key_facts: Stationary cloud attached to a mountain peak, streaming downwind
Forms on the lee side due to orographic lift and eddies
Appears like a flag or banner from the peak
Common on isolated sharp peaks
Indicates orographic forcing and lee turbulence
Icing risk within the cloud

syllabus_desc: Explain the cloud dispersal processes of: direct warming.
concept: Direct warming disperses cloud when the air temperature rises above the dew point, causing the cloud droplets to evaporate. This occurs when solar radiation heats the air (typical dissipation of radiation fog and low stratus after sunrise), or when warm air advects over the cloud layer. As the temperature rises relative to the dew point, relative humidity falls below 100% and the cloud evaporates.
exam_tip: Direct warming: temperature rises above dew point → cloud evaporates. Typical of radiation fog/stratus dispersal after sunrise.
key_facts: Temperature rises above dew point → cloud droplets evaporate
Typical mechanism for morning fog/stratus dispersal
Solar radiation heats air after sunrise
Warm air advection can also warm and disperse cloud
Relative humidity falls below 100% → cloud dissipates

syllabus_desc: Explain the cloud dispersal processes of: sinking of air.
concept: Sinking air warms adiabatically. As air subsides (e.g. in an anticyclone), it descends at the DALR (if unsaturated) or SALR (if saturated) and warms. The warming raises the temperature above the dew point and the cloud evaporates. This is why subsidence inversions in anticyclones produce clear skies below the inversion — the sinking air dissipates any cloud in the subsiding layer.
exam_tip: Sinking air warms adiabatically → temperature rises above dew point → cloud evaporates. Common in anticyclones (subsidence).
key_facts: Sinking (subsiding) air warms adiabatically
Warming raises temperature above dew point
Cloud droplets evaporate
Common mechanism in anticyclones (subsidence)
Subsidence inversions produce clear skies below the inversion

syllabus_desc: Explain the cloud dispersal processes of: mixing with clear air.
concept: When a cloud layer mixes with adjacent clear (unsaturated) dry air, the mixture may no longer be saturated. The dry air lowers the relative humidity of the mixture below 100%, causing the cloud droplets to evaporate. This process is important at cloud edges (erosion by entrainment), in turbulent boundary layers where dry air aloft is mixed downward, and when wind-driven mixing breaks up fog layers.
exam_tip: Mixing with clear dry air → lowers RH below 100% → cloud evaporates. Important at cloud edges (entrainment) and in turbulent mixing.
key_facts: Mixing cloud air with drier, unsaturated clear air
Mixture RH falls below 100% → cloud evaporates
Occurs at cloud edges through entrainment
Turbulent boundary layer mixes dry air downward
Wind increases mixing → accelerates cloud dispersal

syllabus_desc: Describe the Bergeron theory of rainfall development.
concept: The Bergeron (or Bergeron-Findeisen) theory explains precipitation formation in mixed-phase clouds (containing both ice crystals and supercooled water droplets at temperatures between 0°C and −40°C). The saturation vapour pressure over ice is lower than over supercooled water at the same temperature. Ice crystals therefore grow at the expense of the surrounding supercooled water droplets, which evaporate. The ice crystals grow rapidly, become heavy, and fall. As they fall into warmer air (above 0°C), they melt into raindrops. This theory explains most precipitation in mid-latitude clouds, including summer convective rainfall in NZ.
exam_tip: Bergeron theory: ice crystals grow at expense of supercooled water droplets (SVP over ice < SVP over water). Ice crystals fall, melt → rain. Explains most mid-latitude precipitation.
key_facts: Requires mixed cloud: ice crystals AND supercooled water droplets
Temperature range: 0°C to −40°C
SVP over ice < SVP over supercooled water at same temperature
Water droplets evaporate; ice crystals grow
Ice crystals become heavy, fall (snowflakes)
Melt into raindrops if temperature above 0°C on descent
Explains most mid-latitude rainfall

syllabus_desc: Describe the Coalescence theory of rainfall development.
concept: The Coalescence (or Warm Cloud) theory explains precipitation in warm clouds (entirely above 0°C) that lack ice crystals — common in tropical regions. Large cloud droplets (produced on giant condensation nuclei such as sea salt) fall faster than smaller droplets and collide and coalesce with them, growing rapidly. Once droplets reach 0.5 mm diameter they are classified as drizzle; above 0.5 mm they are rain. The size difference between droplets is key — it drives the differential fall speed. Turbulence within the cloud increases collision rates and enhances coalescence.
exam_tip: Coalescence: large droplets fall faster, collide with small droplets and merge, growing into raindrops. Warm cloud process (no ice). Important in tropics.
key_facts: Requires size difference between cloud droplets
Large droplets fall faster than small droplets
Collision and coalescence: droplets merge and grow
Giant condensation nuclei (sea salt) produce large initial droplets
Turbulence increases collision rate
Drizzle: >0.2 mm; Rain: >0.5 mm diameter
Main precipitation process in tropical (warm) clouds

syllabus_desc: List the factors that affect the fall rate of water droplets.
concept: The fall rate (terminal velocity) of a water droplet through air increases with: (1) Droplet size — larger drops fall faster (terminal velocity increases with diameter). (2) Air density — lower density at altitude → faster fall rate (less drag). (3) Temperature — warmer air is less dense → higher fall rate. The fall rate decreases with: (1) Smaller droplet size — small drizzle droplets fall very slowly (~0.5 m/s). (2) Higher air density — denser air provides more drag. Updrafts within cumulonimbus can exceed the terminal velocity of even large hailstones, keeping them aloft for multiple cycles of growth.
exam_tip: Fall rate increases with: droplet size (main factor), lower air density (altitude), warmer temperature. Decreases with: smaller droplet size, higher air density.
key_facts: Larger droplets fall faster (terminal velocity ∝ diameter)
Lower air density (higher altitude) → faster fall
Warmer air (less dense) → faster fall
Small drizzle droplets: ~0.5 m/s fall rate
Large raindrops: ~9 m/s fall rate
Updrafts in Cb can exceed terminal velocity → hail growth cycles

syllabus_desc: Define runway visual range (RVR).
concept: Runway Visual Range (RVR) is the range over which the pilot of an aircraft on the centre-line of a runway can see the runway surface markings or the lights delineating the runway or identifying its centre-line. RVR is measured by transmissometers located alongside the runway (at threshold, touchdown zone, mid-point and stop-end). It is used for low-visibility operations and reported in metres. RVR is more operationally relevant than general meteorological visibility because it measures conditions directly applicable to the runway environment.
exam_tip: RVR: range pilot on runway centreline can see runway markings/lights. Measured by transmissometers. Reported in metres. More relevant than met visibility for runway operations.
key_facts: Range pilot can see runway markings/lights from runway centreline
Measured by transmissometers along the runway
Reported in metres
Used for low-visibility procedures (CAT I/II/III)
More operationally relevant than general visibility
Positions: threshold, touchdown zone, mid-point, stop-end

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of altitude on visibility.
concept: Visibility generally improves with altitude because the main visibility-reducing agents (water vapour, aerosols, dust, smoke, pollutants) are concentrated near the surface. As altitude increases: aerosol and pollution concentrations decrease; the air is drier (less humidity-related haze); dust and smoke from surface sources are left behind. However, visibility can be reduced at altitude by: cloud (in IMC), volcanic ash, high-altitude smoke layers, and Saharan dust transported aloft. In NZ, visibility above the temperature inversion in an anticyclone can be excellent.
exam_tip: Visibility improves with altitude (fewer aerosols, drier air, less pollution). Exceptions: cloud, volcanic ash, high-altitude smoke layers.
key_facts: Visibility generally increases with altitude
Aerosols and pollutants concentrated near the surface
Drier air at altitude → less humidity haze
Dust and surface smoke sources left below
Exceptions: cloud (IMC), volcanic ash, high-altitude smoke/dust
Above subsidence inversion in high: excellent visibility

syllabus_desc: State the difference between fog, mist and haze.
concept: Fog: visibility reduced to less than 1,000 m by water droplets (RH at or near 100%). Mist: visibility reduced to 1,000 m to 5,000 m by water droplets (RH typically 95–100%). Haze: visibility reduced by dry particles — dust, smoke, salt crystals (RH below 75%). The key distinction is: fog and mist are water droplet phenomena (humidity-related), while haze is a dry particle phenomenon. METAR reports use FG (fog, vis <1,000 m), BR (mist, vis 1,000–5,000 m), and HZ (haze).
exam_tip: Fog: vis <1,000 m, water droplets, RH ~100%. Mist: 1,000–5,000 m, water droplets, RH 95–100%. Haze: dry particles (dust/smoke/salt), RH <75%.
key_facts: Fog: visibility <1,000 m, water droplets, RH ≈ 100%
Mist: visibility 1,000–5,000 m, water droplets, RH 95–100%
Haze: visibility reduced by dry particles, RH typically <75%
Fog and mist = water droplet phenomena (humidity)
Haze = dry particle phenomenon (dust, smoke, salt)
METAR codes: FG (fog), BR (mist), HZ (haze)

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of formation of radiation and advection fog with respect to: required meteorological conditions.
concept: Radiation fog requires: clear skies (for effective long-wave radiation cooling), light winds (2–5 kts — calm suppresses fog formation, strong winds prevent it forming), high relative humidity (close to dew point), and a moist surface. Advection fog requires: warm moist air moving over a cooler surface, moderate winds (5–15 kts to maintain advection without disrupting the fog), and a surface cool enough to chill the air to its dew point. Key difference: radiation fog forms at night from cooling in place; advection fog forms when air moves over a cool surface.
exam_tip: Radiation fog: clear sky + light wind (2–5 kt) + high RH + night. Advection fog: warm moist air over cool surface + moderate wind (5–15 kt).
key_facts: Radiation fog: clear skies (radiation cooling), light winds 2–5 kts, high RH, night-time
Calm winds suppress radiation fog (no turbulent mixing to cool deep layer)
Strong winds prevent radiation fog (too much mixing)
Advection fog: warm moist air advects over cool surface
Advection fog: moderate winds 5–15 kts maintain advection
Advection fog: surface must be cool enough to chill air to dew point

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of formation of radiation and advection fog with respect to: factors affecting the extent of the fog.
concept: Radiation fog extent: larger where humidity is high over a wide area, in valleys (cold air drainage concentrates fog), over moist ground; restricted where terrain causes drainage flow. Advection fog extent: larger where the cool surface is extensive (e.g. cold ocean current, large snowfield), and where winds advect the warm moist air over a wide area. Radiation fog is typically patchy (follows terrain); advection fog is more uniform and extensive.
exam_tip: Radiation fog: patchy, follows terrain (valleys). Advection fog: uniform, extensive (follows cool surface extent). Both larger with higher humidity over wider area.
key_facts: Radiation fog: patchy, concentrated in valleys (cold air drainage)
Advection fog: uniform and extensive (follows cool surface)
Both fog types: larger with higher atmospheric humidity
Radiation fog localised by terrain; advection fog ignores terrain more
Cold ocean currents generate extensive advection fog banks

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of formation of radiation and advection fog with respect to: factors affecting the timing of the fog.
concept: Radiation fog forms at night — typically 2–4 hours after sunset as the surface cools to the dew point; fog thickens and deepens through the night; deepest just after dawn; disperses after sunrise (1–3 hours after, depending on cloud cover and insolation). Advection fog timing depends on when the warm moist air arrives over the cold surface — it can occur at any time of day or night and persists as long as the advection continues.
exam_tip: Radiation fog: forms 2–4 hrs after sunset, deepest at dawn, disperses 1–3 hrs after sunrise. Advection fog: any time of day/night while advection continues.
key_facts: Radiation fog: forms 2–4 hours after sunset
Radiation fog: deepest just after dawn
Radiation fog: disperses 1–3 hours after sunrise
Advection fog: can form at any time of day or night
Advection fog: persists while warm moist air advection continues
Advection fog: does not depend on time of day

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of formation of radiation and advection fog with respect to: factors affecting the dispersal of the fog.
concept: Radiation fog dispersal: solar heating after sunrise raises temperature above dew point, evaporating the fog; increasing wind speed (above 8–10 kts) lifts and disperses it as low stratus; cloud cover delays dispersal by reducing insolation. Advection fog dispersal: cessation of warm moist air advection (wind change); warming of the underlying surface; increasing wind speed breaking up the fog layer; or the fog being advected out of the area.
exam_tip: Radiation fog dispersal: sunshine + temperature rise; or wind speed increase (>8–10 kt) lifts to stratus. Advection fog: wind change, surface warming, or advection out of area.
key_facts: Radiation fog: solar heating raises temp above dew point → dispersal
Radiation fog: wind speed >8–10 kts lifts to low stratus
Cloud cover delays radiation fog dispersal (blocks insolation)
Advection fog: wind change stops advection → dispersal
Advection fog: warming of underlying surface
Advection fog: increasing wind speed or advection out of area

syllabus_desc: Define 'super-cooled water droplets'.
concept: Super-cooled water droplets are liquid water droplets that exist at temperatures below 0°C without freezing. They remain liquid because they lack ice nuclei — without a surface to nucleate ice crystal growth, water can remain liquid down to approximately −40°C (below which spontaneous freezing occurs). When a super-cooled droplet contacts an object (aircraft surface), the impact shock provides the nucleation site and the droplet freezes instantly. This is the primary mechanism for airframe icing. Super-cooled droplets are found in clouds between 0°C and −40°C.
exam_tip: Supercooled droplets: liquid water below 0°C (lack ice nuclei). Stable down to ~−40°C. Freeze instantly on contact with aircraft — primary icing mechanism.
key_facts: Liquid water droplets existing below 0°C
Remain liquid due to absence of ice nuclei
Stable down to approximately −40°C
Below −40°C: spontaneous freezing occurs
Freeze instantly on contact with aircraft surface
Found in clouds between 0°C and −40°C
Primary cause of airframe icing

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: clear (glaze) ice.
concept: Clear (glaze) ice forms when large supercooled water droplets (typically in cumulonimbus, heavy rain clouds, or large drops in moderate temperature ranges of 0°C to −10°C) impact the aircraft surface. The droplets are large enough that they do not freeze instantly — the water spreads out as a film over the leading edge and freezes gradually, creating a smooth, clear, dense ice layer. It is the most dangerous icing type due to its weight, aerodynamic disruption, poor detectability (hard to see), and strong adhesion to the surface.
exam_tip: Clear ice: large supercooled drops in 0°C to −10°C. Droplets spread before freezing → smooth, clear, dense ice. Most dangerous icing type — heavy, hard to see, strong adhesion.
key_facts: Forms from large supercooled water droplets
Temperature range: 0°C to −10°C (most common)
Drops too large to freeze instantly — spread as film
Results in smooth, clear, dense, glassy ice
Most dangerous icing type
Heavy weight, aerodynamic disruption, hard to see, strong adhesion
Associated with Cb, heavy rain, and warm front cloud

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: rime (opaque) ice.
concept: Rime (opaque) ice forms when small supercooled water droplets (typically in stratiform clouds, at colder temperatures −10°C to −20°C) impact the aircraft surface and freeze almost instantly on contact. Air is trapped between the tiny droplets as they freeze, creating a white, opaque, porous, brittle ice structure. The ice accretion tends to be pointed and grows into the airstream. Rime ice is less aerodynamically disruptive per unit thickness than clear ice but can affect lift if it accumulates on leading edges.
exam_tip: Rime ice: small supercooled drops at −10°C to −20°C freeze instantly → white, opaque, brittle, porous ice. Less dense than clear ice. Grows into airstream.
key_facts: Forms from small supercooled water droplets
Temperature range: −10°C to −20°C (most common)
Droplets freeze instantly on contact
Air trapped between frozen droplets → white, opaque, porous structure
Brittle and less dense than clear ice
Grows in the direction of the airflow (into airstream)
Less dangerous than clear ice per unit thickness

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: mixed ice.
concept: Mixed ice is a combination of clear and rime ice, forming when conditions vary between those producing each type — typically in the temperature range of −10°C to −15°C with a mixture of large and small supercooled droplets. It creates an irregular, rough ice surface that combines the weight and adhesion of clear ice with the rough texture of rime ice. Mixed ice is common in cumulonimbus and frontal cloud where temperature and drop size vary rapidly with altitude.
exam_tip: Mixed ice: combination of clear and rime ice, −10°C to −15°C, mixed drop sizes. Irregular rough surface. Common in Cb and frontal cloud.
key_facts: Combination of clear and rime ice characteristics
Temperature range: −10°C to −15°C
Mixed large and small supercooled droplets
Irregular, rough ice surface
Combines weight of clear ice with roughness of rime
Common in Cb and frontal cloud

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: hoar frost.
concept: Hoar frost forms when water vapour deposits directly as ice crystals on a surface whose temperature is at or below 0°C — this is sublimation (vapour to solid, bypassing liquid phase). It requires the surface to be below 0°C and the air to be at or above the frost point. Hoar frost produces a white, feathery, crystalline coating on aircraft surfaces. It is most commonly found on aircraft that have been parked overnight in cold, clear, calm conditions, or when a cold-soaked aircraft descends into warmer, moist air. Hoar frost on wings is hazardous — it disrupts laminar flow and increases stall speed.
exam_tip: Hoar frost: water vapour deposits directly as ice crystals (sublimation) on surface ≤0°C. White feathery crystals. Cold-soaked aircraft, overnight parking. Disrupts laminar flow.
key_facts: Water vapour deposits directly as ice crystals (sublimation)
Requires surface temperature ≤0°C
White, feathery, crystalline appearance
Common on cold-soaked aircraft or overnight parked aircraft
Also forms when cold aircraft descends into warm moist air
Disrupts laminar flow — increases stall speed
Must be removed before flight ("cold and bright = hazard" rule

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: freezing rain.
concept: Freezing rain forms when raindrops (liquid water) fall from a warm air layer aloft through a surface layer below 0°C and arrive as super-cooled water at the surface. The drops freeze on contact with any surface at or below 0°C, creating a glaze of clear ice. In the atmosphere, freezing rain typically occurs in warm front conditions where a surface cold pool is overlaid by warm moist air. The drops travel through the cold surface air too quickly to refreeze, arriving as large supercooled drops. Freezing rain causes the most severe airframe icing — large, liquid drops freeze as clear ice, accumulating rapidly.
exam_tip: Freezing rain: rain falls from warm air above into sub-0°C surface layer, arrives as supercooled liquid and freezes on contact. Most severe icing scenario. Warm front situation.
key_facts: Rain falls from warm layer above into cold layer below
Arrives at surface as supercooled (liquid) drops
Freezes instantly on contact with surface ≤0°C
Creates severe glaze (clear) ice
Typical warm front situation (warm-over-cold)
Most severe icing scenario for aircraft
Large supercooled drops freeze rapidly on aircraft

topic: Icing Effect on Lift and Stall
syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe the following: associated cloud types.
concept: Clear ice: primarily associated with cumulonimbus (Cb), deep stratocumulus, nimbostratus in warm front conditions, and freezing rain below frontal cloud. Rime ice: primarily associated with stratiform cloud types — stratus, stratocumulus, altostratus, and nimbostratus at colder temperatures. Mixed ice: associated with deep frontal cloud, cumulonimbus where temperature varies rapidly with altitude, and altocumulus.
exam_tip: Clear ice: Cb, deep Sc, Ns (warm front). Rime ice: St, Sc, As, Ns (cold temps). Mixed ice: deep frontal cloud, Cb.
key_facts: Clear ice: Cb, deep Sc, Ns in warm front, freezing rain
Rime ice: St, Sc, As, Ns at colder temperatures
Mixed ice: deep frontal cloud, Cb, Ac
Clear ice associated with large-drop clouds
