Rime ice associated with fine-drop stratiform cloud

syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe the following: temperature ranges.
concept: Clear ice: most prevalent 0°C to −10°C where large supercooled droplets exist and cannot freeze instantly. Rime ice: most prevalent −10°C to −20°C where small droplets freeze on impact. Mixed ice: most prevalent −10°C to −15°C where both conditions exist. Below −20°C: icing decreases significantly as the atmosphere holds less supercooled water; below −40°C virtually no supercooled water exists (spontaneous freezing).
exam_tip: Clear ice: 0°C to −10°C. Rime ice: −10°C to −20°C. Mixed: −10°C to −15°C. Below −20°C icing decreases. Below −40°C virtually no icing.
key_facts: Clear ice: 0°C to −10°C
Rime ice: −10°C to −20°C
Mixed ice: −10°C to −15°C
Peak icing zone overall: 0°C to −20°C
Icing decreases significantly below −20°C
Virtually no supercooled water below −40°C

topic: Icing Effect on Weight and CG
syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe the following: droplet size.
concept: Clear ice: large droplets (diameter >50 micrometres) — too large to freeze instantly, spread as a film before freezing. Rime ice: small droplets (diameter <20 micrometres) — freeze instantly on contact, trap air. Mixed ice: mixed range of droplet sizes. Droplet size determines whether the water has time to spread (clear) or freezes in place (rime). Large droplets come from warm, deep clouds; small droplets from stratiform cloud layers.
exam_tip: Clear ice: large drops (>50 µm) spread before freezing. Rime ice: small drops (<20 µm) freeze instantly. Droplet size = key determinant of ice type.
key_facts: Clear ice: large droplets >50 micrometres
Rime ice: small droplets <20 micrometres
Mixed ice: range of droplet sizes
Large drops spread as film before freezing → smooth clear ice
Small drops freeze instantly → trap air → white rime ice
Large drops from warm deep clouds; small from stratiform

topic: Icing Effect on Engines and Propellers
syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe the following: height range relative to the freezing level.
concept: Clear ice: primarily near and just above the 0°C level (freezing level) where temperatures are 0°C to −10°C — i.e. just above the freezing level. Rime ice: above the freezing level in colder air, at −10°C to −20°C — typically 3,000–8,000 ft above the freezing level depending on the lapse rate. Mixed ice: intermediate heights between clear and rime zones, at the transition altitude. The actual heights depend on the prevailing lapse rate and freezing level altitude.
exam_tip: Clear ice: just above the freezing level (0°C to −10°C zone). Rime ice: higher up (−10°C to −20°C zone). Mixed: between the two zones.
key_facts: Clear ice: just above the freezing level (0°C to −10°C)
Rime ice: further above the freezing level (−10°C to −20°C)
Mixed ice: intermediate heights between clear and rime zones
Actual heights depend on atmospheric lapse rate
Freezing level defines the base of the icing zone

topic: Icing Effect on Instruments and Systems
syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe the following: enhancing factors.
concept: Factors that enhance icing severity: (1) High liquid water content (LWC) of the cloud — more supercooled water available. (2) Large droplet size — more water per impact. (3) High airspeed — more droplets intercepted per second. (4) Larger wing chord (larger leading edge collection area). (5) Prolonged flight time in icing conditions. (6) Thick deep cloud with high LWC. (7) Active convection (Cb) producing large supercooled drops. (8) Temperature just below 0°C where LWC is highest.
exam_tip: Icing enhanced by: high liquid water content, large drops, high airspeed, larger wing chord, extended time in cloud, Cb clouds, temperature just below 0°C.
key_facts: High liquid water content (LWC) — more supercooled water
Large droplet size — more water per impact
High airspeed — more droplets intercepted per second
Larger wing chord — larger leading edge collection area
Prolonged exposure time in icing cloud
Temperature just below 0°C (maximum LWC)
Active convection (Cb) producing large supercooled drops

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors that influence the rate of ice accretion.
concept: Ice accretion rate is influenced by: (1) Liquid Water Content (LWC) — primary factor; higher LWC = faster accretion. (2) Droplet size — larger drops = faster accretion (clear ice). (3) Airspeed — higher speed = more droplets collected per second. (4) Temperature — just below 0°C gives highest LWC; accretion decreases below −20°C. (5) Aircraft size/shape — larger collection surfaces accumulate more ice; sharp leading edges accumulate less than blunt ones. (6) Time in icing conditions — cumulative exposure.
exam_tip: Rate of accretion: primary factor = Liquid Water Content. Also: drop size, airspeed, temperature (just below 0°C = worst), aircraft geometry, exposure time.
key_facts: Liquid Water Content (LWC): primary driver of accretion rate
Larger droplet size: faster accretion (clear ice)
Higher airspeed: more droplets intercepted per second
Temperature just below 0°C: highest LWC and fastest accretion
Accretion decreases significantly below −20°C
Larger/blunter surfaces accumulate more ice
Cumulative exposure time in icing cloud

syllabus_desc: Describe the hazards of airframe icing to aircraft in flight.
concept: Airframe icing hazards: (1) Reduced lift — ice disrupts the aerofoil's smooth upper surface, causing flow separation and reducing CL max; stall speed increases. (2) Increased drag — ice increases profile drag significantly. (3) Increased weight — ice accumulation adds weight, requiring more lift. (4) Reduced thrust — ice on propellers reduces efficiency; engine intakes blocked. (5) Control problems — ice on control surfaces causes restricted movement, altered hinge moments, or aerodynamic imbalance. (6) Blocked pitot/static — erroneous airspeed and altimeter readings. (7) Radio antenna icing — communication loss. (8) Windscreen icing — nil forward visibility.
exam_tip: Icing hazards: reduced lift (flow separation), increased drag, added weight, control restriction, blocked pitot/static (false instruments), engine/prop icing, windscreen icing.
key_facts: Reduced lift: ice disrupts aerofoil, reduces CL max, raises stall speed
Increased drag: profile drag increases significantly
Added weight: ice weight requires more lift
Control restriction: ice on control surfaces
Blocked pitot: false airspeed indication
Blocked static: false altimeter/VSI readings
Engine intake/carburettor icing
Propeller icing: vibration and reduced thrust
Windscreen icing: zero forward visibility

syllabus_desc: List the intensity classifications of icing.
concept: ICAO icing intensity classifications: Trace — ice barely perceptible; rate of accumulation slightly greater than sublimation; not hazardous unless encountered for extended period. Light — rate of accumulation may create problems if flight exceeds one hour; deicing/anti-icing equipment removes it. Moderate — rate of accumulation is hazardous; diversion necessary even for short exposure; deicing/anti-icing may not cope. Severe — rate of accumulation is such that deicing/anti-icing equipment fails to reduce or control the hazard; immediate diversion essential.
exam_tip: ICAO icing: Trace → Light → Moderate → Severe. Severe: deicing fails to cope; immediate diversion required.
key_facts: Trace: barely perceptible; not hazardous for short periods
Light: may be hazardous after 1+ hour; deicing handles it
Moderate: hazardous even short exposure; diversion necessary
Severe: deicing fails; immediate diversion essential
ICAO standard classification used in PIREPs and forecasts

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of different intensity classifications of icing on aircraft.
concept: Trace icing: very slow build-up; de-icing boots or anti-icing systems easily manage it; not immediately hazardous. Light icing: gradual build-up; routine use of de-ice/anti-ice required; performance degradation beginning. Moderate icing: significant performance degradation; increased stall speed; increased drag; difficulty maintaining altitude; de-icing may not fully cope; diversion strongly advised. Severe icing: rapid accumulation exceeds all aircraft protection systems; rapid and significant performance loss; increased stall speed may exceed manoeuver margins; potential loss of control; immediate diversion and descent to warmer air is essential.
exam_tip: Trace: managed easily. Light: de-ice required, some performance loss. Moderate: significant performance hit, divert. Severe: exceeds all protection, immediate divert, risk of loss of control.
key_facts: Trace: minor, easily managed by deicing systems
Light: deicing required, minor performance degradation
Moderate: significant stall speed increase, drag increase, divert
Severe: rapid accumulation exceeds all aircraft protection
Severe: immediate diversion and descent essential
Severe: risk of loss of control if not treated immediately

syllabus_desc: Explain methods of avoiding or mitigating airframe icing.
concept: Avoidance methods: (1) Pre-flight planning — check PIREPs, SIGMETs, icing forecasts; avoid routes with icing risk. (2) Route/altitude selection — fly above or below icing layer; avoid cloud in 0°C to −20°C temperature range. (3) Minimise time in icing conditions — expedite through if unavoidable. Mitigation methods: (4) Anti-icing systems — prevent ice forming (heated leading edges, TKS fluid, pneumatic boots pre-inflation). (5) De-icing systems — remove ice after it forms (pneumatic boots, heated surfaces). (6) Recognise and respond — monitor ice build-up; if moderate/severe, divert immediately. (7) Descent to warmer air — most effective escape if terrain allows.
exam_tip: Avoidance: pre-flight planning (PIREPs/SIGMETs), altitude selection above/below icing layer. Mitigation: anti-ice/de-ice systems, minimise exposure, descend to warmer air.
key_facts: Pre-flight: check PIREPs, SIGMETs, TAFs, icing forecasts
Route/altitude selection: above or below icing layer
Avoid 0°C to −20°C temperature range in visible moisture
Anti-icing: prevents ice forming (heated edges, TKS, boots)
De-icing: removes ice after formation (boots, heated surfaces)
Minimise time in icing — expedite transit
Immediate diversion if moderate/severe icing
Descent to warmer air: most effective escape

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions required for the development of thunderstorms.
concept: Three conditions are required for thunderstorm development: (1) A deep layer of conditionally unstable air — the ELR must be between the SALR and DALR so that once a parcel is lifted to its LCL, it becomes buoyant and continues to rise. (2) Sufficient moisture — for condensation to occur and release latent heat (fuel for the storm). (3) A lifting mechanism (trigger) — to initiate the initial ascent to the LCL: surface heating (daytime), orographic lift, frontal convergence, or upper-level divergence. CAPE (Convective Available Potential Energy) quantifies the energy available — high CAPE = severe thunderstorm potential.
exam_tip: Thunderstorm needs: (1) conditional instability, (2) sufficient moisture, (3) a trigger (surface heating, orographic, frontal). High CAPE = severe storm potential.
key_facts: Conditionally unstable atmosphere (ELR between SALR and DALR)
Sufficient moisture for condensation and latent heat release
Trigger mechanism: surface heating, orographic, frontal convergence
CAPE = Convective Available Potential Energy (energy available)
High CAPE + trigger = severe thunderstorm potential
CIN (Convective Inhibition) must be overcome for storm initiation

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: convective localised (stationary) thunderstorms.
concept: Convective localised (airmass) thunderstorms develop from strong surface heating on summer afternoons over land. They are triggered by thermals breaking through a capping inversion. They are relatively small (10–50 km diameter), isolated, and stationary (following the weak synoptic wind slowly). They go through all three stages (cumulus, mature, dissipating) in 30–60 minutes and are usually not severe. They typically occur inland in the afternoon when CAPE is highest. In NZ, these are common over the interior of the North Island and South Island on summer days.
exam_tip: Convective localised storms: summer afternoon surface heating, small and isolated, 30–60 min life cycle, airmass storms, not usually severe.
key_facts: Triggered by surface heating (daytime thermals)
Common in summer afternoons
Small and isolated (10–50 km diameter)
Relatively slow movement (follows weak synoptic wind)
Life cycle: 30–60 minutes total
Go through all three stages
Generally not severe unless CAPE is very high
NZ: common interior North/South Island summer afternoons

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: convective traveling thunderstorms.
concept: Convective traveling (multi-cell or squall) thunderstorms are organised systems that develop when wind shear causes new cells to form on the leading edge of an existing storm's cold outflow. New cells continuously form and old ones dissipate, allowing the system to travel significant distances and persist for many hours. They are generally more severe than single-cell storms and can produce heavy rain, large hail, and strong straight-line winds. They travel with the mean wind and can cover hundreds of kilometres.
exam_tip: Traveling storms: wind shear causes new cells to continuously form. Long-lived, organised systems. More severe than single-cell. Can travel hundreds of km.
key_facts: Wind shear causes new cells to form on outflow boundary
Continuously regenerating: new cells form as old dissipate
Travel with mean wind over large distances
More severe than isolated single-cell storms
Produce heavy rain, hail, strong straight-line winds
Can persist for many hours

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: orographic thunderstorms.
concept: Orographic thunderstorms form when moist unstable air is forced to rise over terrain — mountains, hills, or ridges. The orographic lifting provides the trigger to push air through the LCL. They tend to be stationary over or just downwind of the terrain feature that triggers them and are common on the windward side of mountain ranges in humid conditions. In NZ, orographic thunderstorms are common over the Tararua and Ruahine Ranges and the Southern Alps when warm, moist, unstable air flows from the northwest or northeast.
exam_tip: Orographic thunderstorms: terrain forces moist unstable air to rise. Stationary over/downwind of terrain. NZ: common over mountain ranges in humid NW or NE flow.
key_facts: Triggered by orographic (terrain) lifting
Stationary or slow-moving over/near terrain feature
Require moist, unstable air flow over terrain
Common on windward side of mountain ranges
NZ: Tararua/Ruahine/Southern Alps in NW or NE flow
Can be severe with strong upper winds

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: nocturnal tropical thunderstorms.
concept: Nocturnal tropical thunderstorms occur at night over tropical oceans, driven by radiative cooling of the cloud tops which destabilises the upper cloud layers and re-initiates convection. They are most common in the tropics (0–20° latitude) over warm ocean surfaces. They are not driven by surface heating (which is a daytime process) but by cloud-top cooling. They are often organised into mesoscale convective systems (MCS) and can produce severe weather. Their nocturnal timing means they can be encountered on overnight routes across tropical oceans.
exam_tip: Nocturnal tropical storms: driven by cloud-top radiative cooling at night over warm tropical oceans. Not surface-heating driven. Often organised MCS. Overnight flight hazard.
key_facts: Triggered by radiative cooling of cloud tops at night
Not driven by surface heating
Common in tropics over warm ocean (0–20° latitude)
Often organised as mesoscale convective systems (MCS)
Can produce severe weather: heavy rain, hail, lightning
Hazard on overnight tropical over-ocean routes

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: frontal and convergence-type thunderstorms.
concept: Frontal thunderstorms develop along cold fronts, warm fronts, and surface convergence zones where lifting is provided by the frontal dynamics. Cold front thunderstorms are the most severe — the sharp cold front undercuts warm moist air, providing rapid lifting. They typically form in a line along the front and can be extensive squall lines. Warm front thunderstorms are often embedded in stratiform cloud (embedded thunderstorms) making them particularly hazardous as they are invisible on visual inspection. Convergence thunderstorms form along low-pressure troughs and sea breeze fronts.
exam_tip: Frontal thunderstorms: cold front (most severe, linear squall line), warm front (embedded in stratus — hidden hazard), convergence zones (troughs, sea breeze fronts).
key_facts: Cold front: most severe frontal thunderstorms, squall lines
Cold front: sharp lifting as cold air undercuts warm moist air
Warm front: thunderstorms embedded in stratiform cloud
Embedded thunderstorms: most dangerous — not visible externally
Convergence zones: surface troughs, sea breeze fronts
Frontal storms can be extensive lines of cells

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: surface trough and upper trough thunderstorms.
concept: Surface trough thunderstorms develop in convergence zones associated with elongated low-pressure troughs at the surface, where converging air masses provide the lifting mechanism. Upper trough thunderstorms are associated with upper-level troughs (cold pools aloft or short-wave troughs) which destabilise the atmosphere by bringing cold air aloft over warmer surface air — increasing the ELR to conditional instability. Upper troughs are invisible at the surface but their effects are seen as organised lines or clusters of thunderstorms.
exam_tip: Surface trough: convergence in surface trough lines lifts unstable air. Upper trough: cold air aloft increases ELR → conditional instability → organised storm clusters.
key_facts: Surface trough: convergence provides lifting mechanism
Surface trough storms: along elongated pressure trough
Upper trough: cold air aloft destabilises atmosphere
Upper trough storms: organised lines or clusters
Upper troughs not visible at surface but cause widespread instability

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: warm front embedded thunderstorms.
concept: Embedded thunderstorms in warm frontal cloud are particularly hazardous because they are hidden within continuous stratiform cloud and cannot be identified visually or detected without onboard weather radar. They develop where pockets of conditional instability exist within the warm frontal zone. The surrounding stratus cloud prevents the pilot from seeing the Cb and the smooth external appearance of the cloud gives no warning. Penetrating embedded thunderstorms without radar is extremely dangerous. Aircraft must use weather radar and AIRMETs/SIGMETs to avoid them.
exam_tip: Warm front embedded Cb: hidden in stratiform cloud, invisible without radar. Most dangerous type — no visual warning. Requires onboard radar to detect and avoid.
key_facts: Hidden within continuous stratiform warm frontal cloud
Invisible to pilots without onboard weather radar
No external visual indication of the embedded Cb
Develop in pockets of instability in the warm frontal zone
Most dangerous thunderstorm type for IFR operations
Requires radar and SIGMETs for avoidance

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: turbulence.
concept: Turbulence in and around thunderstorms ranges from moderate to extreme. It is most severe in the mature stage. Areas: inside the cloud (all stages — avoid), beneath the anvil (jet-stream level downdraft, clear air turbulence), beneath the storm (low-level wind shear and gust front), on the upwind side (updraft zone up to 30 km from storm). Severe to extreme turbulence can occur in clear air up to 20 nm from the visible cloud boundary. The gust front can produce severe low-level turbulence ahead of the storm.
exam_tip: Thunderstorm turbulence: inside cloud = extreme. Under anvil = severe CAT. Under storm = severe windshear/gust front. Clear air up to 20 nm from cloud. Avoid within 20 nm.
key_facts: Extreme turbulence inside the storm cloud
Severe CAT beneath the anvil
Severe low-level turbulence in gust front ahead of storm
Moderate to severe turbulence within 20 nm of cloud boundary
Most severe in mature stage
Avoid thunderstorm by at least 20 nm horizontally

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: icing.
concept: Severe icing occurs within thunderstorms wherever supercooled water exists — between 0°C and −40°C. The most severe icing is in the updraft zone where large supercooled drops accumulate. The freezing level within a Cb is difficult to identify and the presence of large supercooled drops in the updraft can cause rapid clear ice accumulation. Above −40°C the Cb is glaciated (ice crystals only — icing ceases to be a problem for accretion but ice crystal icing is a recognised hazard for jet engines). Ice crystal icing can block Pitot systems and engine fuel/bleed systems.
exam_tip: Cb icing: most severe in updraft zone (large supercooled drops, 0°C to −40°C). Above −40°C: glaciated (ice crystals). Ice crystal icing: engine/pitot hazard in upper Cb.
key_facts: Severe icing in updraft zone (large supercooled drops)
0°C to −40°C: supercooled water icing zone
Rapid clear ice accumulation in updraft
Above −40°C: glaciated (ice crystals only)
Ice crystal icing: hazard to jet engines and pitot systems
Worst icing in mature stage updraft column

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: microbursts.
concept: A microburst is an intense localised downdraft that diverges at the surface, producing a sudden and severe windshear. It is found beneath mature and dissipating thunderstorms, particularly those with heavy precipitation. The hazard zone extends to approximately 4 km radius from the centre, lasting 5–15 minutes. The windshear sequence during a microburst encounter on approach: headwind increase (apparent airspeed increase) followed by a powerful downdraft and then a severe tailwind (significant airspeed decrease at low altitude). This sequence can cause loss of control on approach/departure. Most dangerous at low altitude during takeoff and approach.
exam_tip: Microburst: intense localised downdraft diverging at surface. 4 km radius, 5–15 min. Sequence: headwind → downdraft → tailwind. Catastrophic on takeoff/approach.
key_facts: Intense localised downdraft diverging at surface
Beneath mature/dissipating Cb with heavy rain
Hazard zone: ~4 km radius, 5–15 minutes duration
Approach sequence: headwind increase → downdraft → tailwind decrease
Catastrophic on takeoff and approach at low altitude
Wet and dry microbursts both possible

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: first gust (or gust front).
concept: The first gust (gust front) is a surge of cold air that radiates outward from a thunderstorm as the downdraft spreads along the surface. It precedes the main storm by 10–20 km and can arrive suddenly. The gust front causes: a sudden strong gusty wind shift (180° possible), a temperature drop of 5–10°C, a pressure surge (a trough followed by a ridge), turbulence, and wind shear. Aircraft on approach may experience a sudden headwind followed by wind shear and tailwind as the gust front passes. Recognition on the ground: dust roll, dark line on radar.
exam_tip: First gust/gust front: cold air surge ahead of storm by 10–20 km. Sudden wind shift, temp drop 5–10°C, pressure surge. Severe low-level windshear hazard.
key_facts: Cold downdraft air spreads outward from storm at surface
Precedes main storm by 10–20 km
Sudden strong wind shift (up to 180°)
Temperature drop 5–10°C
Pressure surge: trough then ridge
Severe low-level windshear on approach
Visible as dust roll or radar reflectivity line

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: electrical phenomena.
concept: Thunderstorms produce intense electrical activity. In flight, hazards include: direct lightning strike (structural damage, avionics failure, crew flash-blindness, fuel ignition risk), precipitation static (St P — electrical charge build-up causing radio static and compass errors), and St Elmo's Fire (corona discharge — blue/green glow on windscreen, antenna tips). Aircraft are designed to withstand direct lightning strikes but transient effects can disable avionics temporarily. Avoidance is the best strategy. Precipitation static is mitigated by static wicks.
exam_tip: Electrical hazards: direct lightning (structural/avionics), precipitation static (radio interference), St Elmo's Fire (corona discharge). Static wicks reduce precipitation static.
key_facts: Direct lightning: structural damage, avionics failure, flash-blindness
Lightning risk highest in mature stage updraft/downdraft interface
Precipitation static (St P): radio static, compass errors
St Elmo's Fire: corona discharge, blue/green glow
Modern aircraft designed to survive lightning strikes
Static wicks reduce precipitation static build-up
Avoidance is the best protection

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: tornadoes (if any).
concept: Tornadoes may form in association with severe supercell thunderstorms, where intense rotation (a mesocyclone) develops in the updraft. They are rare in New Zealand and the SW Pacific compared to continental areas such as the US Great Plains. When present, they are found beneath the base of the Cb, in the area of maximum rotation. The hazard zone is immediately beneath the tornado — aircraft should maintain at least 20 nm clearance from any thunderstorm that might be tornadic.
exam_tip: Tornadoes: beneath severe supercell Cb mesocyclone. Rare in NZ. Stay 20 nm from any storm. Avoid the Cb base area.
key_facts: Form beneath supercell Cb in rotating updraft (mesocyclone)
Rare in New Zealand and SW Pacific
Most common in continental mid-latitudes
Hazard zone: immediately beneath Cb base
Maintain at least 20 nm from any severe thunderstorm
Do not fly beneath any thunderstorm

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: hail.
concept: Hail forms in the strong updrafts of mature Cb when supercooled water droplets are carried repeatedly above the freezing level and accumulate ice layers. Hail exits the storm through the anvil outflow and can fall up to 20 km ahead of the visible storm cloud — it may be encountered in clear air. Even moderate hail (1–2 cm) can shatter windscreens, damage leading edges, and dent fuselage. In-flight hail is an indicator that the aircraft is in or near a severe thunderstorm. Avoidance by 20 nm reduces the risk.
exam_tip: Hail: forms in strong updrafts (repeated freezing cycles). Exits via anvil, falls up to 20 km ahead of storm in clear air. Can shatter windscreen and damage structure.
key_facts: Forms in strong updrafts — supercooled water freezes in layers
Larger hail = stronger updraft (supported longer)
Exits via anvil — can fall 20 km ahead of visible storm
Can be encountered in clear air ahead of storm
Shatters windscreens, damages leading edges, dents fuselage
Encountering hail = storm is nearby or overhead
Avoidance by 20 nm reduces risk

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: poor visibility.
concept: Poor visibility in thunderstorm areas results from: heavy precipitation (rain curtains, hail), blowing dust in the gust front, precipitation-induced fog beneath the storm, smoke from lightning-induced fires, and the dark appearance of the Cb base. Visibility can drop from CAVOK to near zero within seconds in a gust front or heavy rain shaft. Rain on the windscreen significantly reduces forward visibility even at low precipitation rates. Flying VFR toward a thunderstorm is extremely hazardous — conditions can deteriorate faster than the aircraft can escape.
exam_tip: Poor visibility: heavy rain/hail, blowing dust (gust front), precipitation fog. Can drop to zero in seconds. VFR toward a storm is extremely hazardous.
key_facts: Heavy rain and hail reduce visibility rapidly
Gust front: blowing dust reduces visibility to zero
Precipitation-induced fog beneath storm
Dark Cb base difficult to judge distance
Rain on windscreen reduces forward visibility significantly
VFR toward a storm: conditions can deteriorate faster than aircraft can escape

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of multi-cell thunderstorms.
concept: A multi-cell thunderstorm is an organised convective system consisting of a group of cells at different stages of development, arranged so that new cells form on the upwind flank as older cells dissipate on the downwind side. The system has a lifespan of several hours. Each individual cell goes through the cumulus, mature, and dissipating stages in 30–60 minutes, but the system as a whole persists because the outflow from one cell triggers new cell development. Multi-cell storms produce continuous hazardous weather over a wide area and can travel long distances.
exam_tip: Multi-cell: group of cells at different stages, new cells form upwind as old ones dissipate. Hours-long lifespan. Continuous hazard over wide area.
key_facts: Group of cells at different life cycle stages
New cells form on upwind flank
Old cells dissipate on downwind side
System lifespan: several hours
Individual cell lifespan: 30–60 minutes
Outflow from one cell triggers next cell development
Produces continuous hazardous weather over wide area

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of radar to identify thunderstorms.
concept: Weather radar identifies thunderstorms by measuring the reflectivity of precipitation within the cloud. Higher reflectivity (bright colours on modern displays) indicates heavier precipitation and larger hydrometeors — indicative of more intense convection. Doppler radar measures radial wind velocity, identifying rotation (mesocyclone) and gust fronts. Ground-based radar coverage in NZ is provided by MetService. Onboard airborne weather radar (if fitted) displays precipitation intensity ahead of the aircraft. Limitations: radar detects precipitation, not turbulence directly; dry (below-cloud) turbulence is not shown; attenuation behind heavy precipitation can hide storm cells.
exam_tip: Radar identifies Cb by reflectivity (heavier rain = brighter return). Doppler detects rotation. Limitations: no turbulence detection, attenuation hides cells behind heavy rain.
key_facts: Measures reflectivity of precipitation
Higher reflectivity = heavier precipitation = more intense convection
Doppler radar detects rotation (mesocyclone) and gust fronts
NZ: MetService ground-based radar network
Airborne radar: displays precipitation ahead of aircraft
Limitation: detects precipitation not turbulence directly
Attenuation: heavy rain hides cells behind it ("blind spot")

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions that can be taken by pilots to avoid or minimise the effects of flying in the vicinity of thunderstorms.
concept: Precautions: (1) Pre-flight — check TAFs, METARs, SIGMETs, AIRMETs, radar imagery; plan around known thunderstorm areas. (2) In-flight — maintain at least 20 nm horizontal clearance from storm cells; overfly by at least 1,000 ft per 10 kts of reported tops above highest cloud; do not attempt to fly under storm (gust front, microbursts). (3) If onboard radar — use it actively; avoid red/purple returns by 20+ nm. (4) Obtain PIREPs and updated forecasts. (5) Divert early before storm intensifies. (6) Reduce airspeed to turbulence penetration speed if inadvertent penetration occurs. (7) Never penetrate embedded or unknown thunderstorms in IMC.
exam_tip: Precautions: 20 nm horizontal clearance, overfly with clearance, check SIGMETs/radar, divert early, reduce to turbulence penetration speed if caught inside.
key_facts: Pre-flight: check SIGMETs, radar, TAFs, PIREPs
Maintain 20 nm horizontal clearance from storm cells
Overfly tops with 1,000 ft per 10 kts clearance above tops
Never fly beneath a thunderstorm (microbursts, gust front)
Use onboard radar actively — avoid red returns by 20+ nm
Divert early before situation deteriorates
If inadvertent penetration: reduce to turbulence penetration speed
Never penetrate embedded Cb in IMC without radar

syllabus_desc: Describe anticyclones ('highs') with reference to: their formation processes.
concept: Anticyclones form through: (1) Dynamic/subsidence — upper-level convergence at the top of the troposphere forces air downward; the descending air warms adiabatically creating a high surface pressure. This is the mechanism for subtropical highs and blocking highs. (2) Thermal — surface cooling (e.g. land cooling in winter) makes surface air denser and heavier → surface high forms. This is the mechanism for polar and continental highs. (3) Cold air advection — advection of cold dense air from polar regions builds surface pressure.
exam_tip: Highs form by: (1) upper-level convergence → subsidence → warm dry descending air → surface high. (2) Thermal: surface cooling → dense air → cold high.
key_facts: Dynamic high: upper-level convergence forces air downward
Subsiding air warms adiabatically → surface high pressure
Subtropical highs and blocking highs form this way
Thermal/cold high: surface cooling makes air denser
Cold air advection from polar regions builds surface high
Anticyclones = closed clockwise circulation in Southern Hemisphere

syllabus_desc: Describe anticyclones ('highs') with reference to: pressure patterns and wind flow.
concept: In an anticyclone, isobars form closed curves around the centre of highest pressure. Winds flow clockwise around Southern Hemisphere anticyclones (anticlockwise in NH). Wind speed is proportional to isobar spacing — isobars in highs are typically widely spaced near the centre (light winds) and more closely spaced at the periphery. At the surface, friction deflects the wind slightly outward from the centre (divergence). At altitude, geostrophic flow is purely along the isobars. Outflow at the surface is compensated by subsidence aloft.
exam_tip: SH anticyclone: clockwise winds, widely spaced isobars (light winds), surface divergence outward from centre, subsidence aloft.
key_facts: Clockwise circulation in Southern Hemisphere anticyclone
Isobars widely spaced near centre → light winds
Closer isobars at periphery → stronger winds
Surface: outward-spiralling (divergent) flow due to friction
Divergence at surface compensated by subsidence aloft
Upper-level flow is nearly geostrophic (parallel to isobars)

syllabus_desc: Describe anticyclones ('highs') with reference to: subsidence and subsidence inversions.
concept: Subsidence is the large-scale sinking of air within an anticyclone. As air descends, it warms at the DALR. The descending air originates from the upper troposphere, which is dry. This warm dry descending air creates a temperature inversion — the subsidence inversion — where temperature increases with altitude rather than decreasing. The subsidence inversion: traps moisture, pollutants, and cloud below it; is typically found at 5,000–10,000 ft; creates a visible haze layer below and clear air above. Cloud bases on days with subsidence inversions are often capped at the inversion height.
exam_tip: Subsidence: sinking air warms at DALR (dry) → creates subsidence inversion. Traps moisture/pollutants below. Typically 5,000–10,000 ft. Clear above, hazy below.
key_facts: Air sinks in anticyclones — subsides from upper troposphere
Descending air warms at DALR (dry — no latent heat release)
Creates temperature inversion at the base of subsidence
Subsidence inversion: typically 5,000–10,000 ft
Traps moisture, haze, cloud, and pollutants below
Clear skies and excellent visibility above the inversion
Cloud development capped at the inversion level

syllabus_desc: Describe anticyclones ('highs') with reference to: typical associated weather conditions.
concept: Typical weather in an anticyclone: light and variable winds near the centre; few or no clouds (subsidence suppresses convection); good visibility above any low haze layer; warm in summer (clear skies allow maximum solar heating), cold in winter (clear skies allow maximum radiative cooling); possible radiation fog in winter under clear calm nights; possible sea fog on coasts (cold upwelling or advection); possible stratocumulus layer below subsidence inversion.
exam_tip: Anticyclone weather: light winds, little cloud, good vis above haze. Summer: warm. Winter: cold (radiation fog possible). Sc under inversion possible.
key_facts: Light and variable winds especially near centre
Little or no convective cloud (subsidence inhibits convection)
Good visibility above any haze layer
Warm summers (max solar heating with clear skies)
Cold winters (max radiative cooling with clear skies)
Radiation fog on clear calm winter nights
Possible stratocumulus layer below subsidence inversion

syllabus_desc: Describe the development of 'cold' highs.
concept: Cold highs (thermal highs) are shallow, surface-based pressure systems that form when surface air is cooled and becomes denser. They develop over: (1) Continental interiors in winter (Siberian high, Canadian high) — intense surface cooling over land. (2) Polar regions — permanent cold surface. (3) Snow-covered ground — high albedo reduces surface heating. Cold highs are shallow — wind circulation often reverses with altitude above the cold surface layer. They produce stable, cold, clear conditions at the surface with the cold dense air. Cold highs do not extend through the full depth of the troposphere like dynamic highs.
exam_tip: Cold high: surface-based, forms from surface cooling (continental winter, polar, snow). Shallow — circulation reverses with altitude. Cold, stable, clear.
key_facts: Formed by surface cooling making air denser
Also called thermal highs
Continental interior in winter: most intense examples
Polar and snow-covered regions
Shallow system — does not extend through troposphere
Wind circulation may reverse above the cold surface layer
Cold, stable, clear surface conditions

syllabus_desc: Discuss the hazards associated with anticyclones.
concept: Aviation hazards in anticyclones: (1) Radiation fog — clear calm nights lead to surface cooling to dew point; can be dense and widespread; slow to lift after dawn. (2) Low stratus/stratocumulus — moisture trapped below subsidence inversion; can produce sustained low cloud and poor visibility. (3) Haze — reduced visibility from trapped aerosols below the inversion; can reduce VMC conditions. (4) Wind shear — at the inversion level (turbulent boundary between trapped air and clear air above). (5) Icing in stratus/fog layers. (6) Poor performance — hot days with clear skies cause high density altitude. (7) Complacency — "good weather" lulls pilots into reduced vigilance.
exam_tip: Anticyclone hazards: radiation fog, low Sc under inversion, haze (trapped aerosols), wind shear at inversion, icing in cloud, high density altitude (summer), complacency.
key_facts: Radiation fog on clear calm nights (winter)
Low stratus/stratocumulus below subsidence inversion
Haze reducing visibility (trapped aerosols below inversion)
Wind shear at the inversion level
Icing in stratus and fog layers
High density altitude in summer (clear skies → surface heating)
Complacency — apparent fine weather may hide localised hazards

syllabus_desc: Describe in terms of air-mass movement, the development of: cold fronts.
concept: A cold front is the boundary where advancing cold air displaces warmer air. The cold, dense air wedges under the warm air and forces it to rise steeply. Cold fronts in the SH slope backward with altitude (tilt away from the direction of movement — the cold air wedge is narrow). They are associated with: fast-moving (30–40 kts), sharply-defined weather changes; line of cumulonimbus, towering cumulus, and convective showers; rapid wind veer; significant pressure trough; sharp temperature drop; and a wide band of clear weather after passage.
exam_tip: Cold front: advancing cold air undercuts warm air. Fast-moving, steep slope. Line of Cb/TCu, heavy showers, rapid wind veer, temperature drop after passage.
key_facts: Cold, dense air wedges under warm air
Warm air forced to rise steeply and rapidly
Fast-moving: ~30–40 kts
Narrow zone of intense weather (line of Cb, TCu)
Heavy showers, hail possible, squalls
Rapid wind veer and temperature drop on passage
Cold clear air behind the front
Steep backward slope with altitude

syllabus_desc: Describe in terms of air-mass movement, the development of: warm fronts (warm sectors).
concept: A warm front is the boundary where advancing warm air overrides cooler air. Warm, lighter air rises over the cold air in a shallow slope (1:150 to 1:200 slope). The warm sector is the region between the warm front (ahead) and the cold front (behind) in a wave depression. Warm fronts produce a wide, gradual sequence of cloud and precipitation: high cirrus up to 1,000 km ahead, progressing to cirrostratus, altostratus, nimbostratus with continuous rain and low cloud, and stratus/fog near the front. The weather deteriorates slowly over many hours as the front approaches.
exam_tip: Warm front: warm air overrides cold air at shallow slope. Wide weather sequence ahead: Ci → Cs → As → Ns (rain) → St/fog. Weather deteriorates slowly over hours.
key_facts: Warm, lighter air rises over receding cold air
Shallow slope: 1:150 to 1:200 (gradual rise)
Wide weather sequence up to 1,000 km ahead of surface position
Cloud sequence: Ci → Cs → As → Ns → St/fog
Continuous rain or snow in the Ns zone
Low cloud and poor visibility near the front
Slow, gradual weather deterioration over many hours

syllabus_desc: Describe in terms of air-mass movement, the development of: occluded fronts.
concept: An occluded front forms when the faster-moving cold front catches up with the warm front. The warm air (warm sector) is lifted off the surface entirely. Two types: cold occlusion (cold air behind the cold front is colder than the cold air ahead of the warm front — the cold front undercuts the existing cold air and continues to the surface) and warm occlusion (cold air behind the cold front is less cold than the air ahead — the cold front rides over the pre-existing cold air). Occlusions produce complex, prolonged weather — a mix of cold and warm front characteristics with continuous cloud and precipitation.
exam_tip: Occlusion: cold front catches warm front; warm air lifted completely off surface. Complex prolonged weather. Cold or warm occlusion depending on relative temperatures.
key_facts: Cold front overtakes warm front
Warm sector air lifted completely off the surface
Cold occlusion: cold front air colder than air ahead
Warm occlusion: cold front air less cold than air ahead
Complex, prolonged weather — mix of both front types
Continuous cloud and precipitation in the occlusion zone
Occlusion marks the mature/dying stage of the depression

syllabus_desc: Describe in terms of air-mass movement, the development of: stationary fronts.
concept: A stationary front forms when a frontal boundary between two air masses stalls and does not advance. This occurs when the pressure gradient perpendicular to the front is insufficient to drive movement, or when the two air masses are in balance. Stationary fronts produce prolonged cloud and precipitation along and near the front, as air is lifted along the boundary for extended periods. They can persist for days. A stationary front may oscillate back and forth and can re-activate and move as a warm or cold front when the pressure pattern changes.
exam_tip: Stationary front: frontal boundary stalls — two air masses in balance. Prolonged cloud and rain along front for days. May re-activate as warm or cold front.
key_facts: Frontal boundary not advancing in either direction
Two air masses roughly in balance or insufficient pressure gradient
Prolonged cloud and precipitation along the front
Can persist for days
May oscillate or re-activate as conditions change
Affects the same areas repeatedly with persistent bad weather

syllabus_desc: Outline the characteristics of: mid to high-latitude depressions ('lows').
concept: Mid to high-latitude depressions (extratropical cyclones) are large-scale low-pressure systems (500–3,000 km diameter) that form along frontal boundaries in the mid-latitudes (30–60°). They are characterised by: anticlockwise circulation in the SH; warm sector between warm and cold fronts; frontal weather (Ns rain, Cb squalls); life cycle of 3–7 days. They are the dominant weather-producing systems in NZ. They intensify on the poleward-moving warm sector side and decay when the occlusion process is complete. They travel eastward with the mid-latitude westerlies.
exam_tip: Mid-lat depressions: large (500–3,000 km), anticlockwise SH, frontal weather, 3–7 day life cycle, eastward movement with westerlies. Dominant weather system in NZ.
key_facts: Large-scale: 500–3,000 km diameter
Anticlockwise circulation in Southern Hemisphere
Frontal system: warm front ahead, cold front behind
Warm sector between the fronts
Life cycle: 3–7 days (from wave to occlusion)
Travel eastward with mid-latitude westerlies
Dominant weather-producing systems in New Zealand

syllabus_desc: Outline the characteristics of: sub-tropical depressions.
concept: Sub-tropical depressions form in the subtropical belt (20–35°) and are generally weaker than mid-latitude depressions. They can form from: remnants of tropical cyclones that move poleward and lose their tropical character; from upper-level troughs creating surface convergence; or from thermal heating over land in summer. They typically lack the well-defined frontal structure of mid-latitude depressions and produce more disorganised, convective weather. In the context of NZ, subtropical depressions approaching from the N or NW can bring extended periods of cloud, rain, and humidity.
exam_tip: Sub-tropical depressions: weaker, form 20–35°, less organised than mid-lat, often from tropical remnants or upper troughs. Bring cloudy humid weather to NZ from N/NW.
key_facts: Forms in subtropical belt: 20–35° latitude
Generally weaker than mid-latitude depressions
Sources: tropical cyclone remnants, upper troughs, thermal
Less organised — often lacks clear frontal structure
More convective, disorganised precipitation
Approach NZ from N or NW bringing cloud and rain

syllabus_desc: Outline the characteristics of: tropical cyclones.
concept: Tropical cyclones are intense warm-core, symmetric, low-pressure systems that form over warm tropical oceans (SST >26°C). They have: no fronts (warm core — temperature decreases outward from centre); spiral bands of intense convection; a calm eye at the centre; maximum wind speeds in the eyewall; pressure can fall to 900 hPa or less. They intensify over warm water and weaken rapidly over land or cooler water. They are categorised by wind speed (Beaufort and Saffir-Simpson scales). Tropical cyclones (called typhoons in NW Pacific, hurricanes in Atlantic) affect the SW Pacific from November to April.
exam_tip: Tropical cyclones: warm-core, no fronts, eye + eyewall, intense convection spirals. Form SST >26°C. SW Pacific: November–April. Weaken over land or cold water.
key_facts: Warm-core system: no fronts
Forms over warm ocean: SST >26°C
Eye: calm centre, lowest pressure
Eyewall: maximum winds and most intense convection
Spiral rainbands surround the eye
SW Pacific season: November to April
Weakens rapidly over land or SST <26°C
SH rotation: clockwise

syllabus_desc: Describe the development and the aspects of importance to aviation of: lee depressions.
concept: A lee depression forms on the downwind (lee) side of a mountain range when a mass of air is forced over the barrier. The mountain accelerates the upper flow while creating a surface convergence zone in the lee — this can generate a new low-pressure centre. In NZ, lee depressions commonly form east of the Southern Alps when a trough crosses the South Island from the west. Lee lows can develop rapidly and unexpectedly. For aviation, they bring rapid deterioration: strengthening NW winds on the west coast, SE wind on the east coast near the low, and complex wind shear and turbulence.
exam_tip: Lee depression: low forms on lee (downwind) side of mountains. NZ: commonly east of Southern Alps. Rapid development, NW winds west coast, SE wind east coast.
key_facts: Forms on the lee (downwind) side of a mountain barrier
Upper airflow accelerated over mountains creates surface divergence in lee
NZ: commonly forms east of Southern Alps
Can develop rapidly and unexpectedly
NW winds on west coast; SE near the low on east coast
Complex wind shear and turbulence near the lee low

syllabus_desc: Describe the development and the aspects of importance to aviation of: thermal lows.
concept: Thermal lows form over land surfaces when intense solar heating creates a region of hot, rising air and low surface pressure. The warm rising air decreases surface pressure, forming a closed low. Thermal lows are typically shallow — they weaken rapidly with altitude as the temperature contrast diminishes. They are semi-permanent features over land in summer: the Australian heat low and the NZ interior can develop thermal lows on hot days. Aviation significance: thermal lows produce strong convective turbulence, dust devils (marker of strong thermals), and afternoon convective thunderstorms in unstable conditions.
exam_tip: Thermal low: intense surface heating → rising air → surface low. Shallow, summer feature. Convective turbulence, thermals, possible afternoon thunderstorms.
key_facts: Forms over strongly heated land surface
Hot rising air reduces surface pressure
Shallow system: weakens rapidly with altitude
Summer feature — daytime phenomenon
Convective turbulence and thermals within the thermal low
Dust devils indicate strong thermals
Afternoon thunderstorms in unstable conditions

syllabus_desc: Describe the development and the aspects of importance to aviation of: depressions crossing a mountain barrier.
concept: When a depression crosses a mountain barrier, several effects occur: (1) Blocking - the mountain range may prevent the depression from crossing, forcing it to track around the ends or intensify. (2) Splitting - the low may split into two centres as it crosses. (3) Lee re-development - the original low fills over the mountains and a new lee low forms on the lee side. (4) Fohn effect - as the warm sector air is forced over the mountains, a Fohn wind develops on the lee side. For NZ, Southern Ocean depressions crossing the Southern Alps often produce lee lows, Fohn winds (norwesters), and complex weather patterns.
exam_tip: Depression crossing mountains: blocking, splitting, lee re-development, Föhn effect. NZ Southern Alps: Southern Ocean lows → lee low + nor'wester.
key_facts: Mountain range can block depression movement
Depression may split as it attempts to cross
Lee re-development: new low forms on lee side
Original low fills over the mountains
Föhn wind develops on the lee side of the range
NZ: Southern Ocean depressions → lee lows + nor'wester (Canterbury)

syllabus_desc: Describe the four main formation mechanisms for turbulence: convection.
concept: Convective turbulence results from surface heating creating thermals and updrafts. As the sun heats the surface, packets of warm air rise in columns (thermals), creating an irregular pattern of updrafts and downdrafts. It is most intense in the afternoon when surface heating peaks and is strongest over land with high solar absorption (dark soil, urban areas) and in summer. Convective turbulence is light to moderate below cumulus cloud; severe to extreme within and near large cumulus and cumulonimbus. It is typically most pronounced in the convective boundary layer (up to the base of cumulus cloud).
exam_tip: Convective turbulence: surface heating → thermals (updrafts and downdrafts). Worst afternoon over land. Light/mod below Cu; severe/extreme near Cb.
key_facts: Surface heating creates thermals (warm air parcels rising)
Pattern of updrafts and downdrafts throughout the day
Strongest in the afternoon when surface heating peaks
Stronger over dark surfaces and land than sea
Light to moderate below cumulus cloud base
Severe to extreme within and near Cb
Confined to convective boundary layer (below Cu base)

syllabus_desc: Describe the four main formation mechanisms for turbulence: mechanical processes.
concept: Mechanical turbulence results from wind flowing over and around surface irregularities — buildings, trees, terrain features. The flow separates from the surface, creating eddies and wake turbulence downwind of obstacles. It is strongest in strong winds and increases with surface roughness. Low-level mechanical turbulence is a hazard during approach and landing, particularly in crosswind conditions where hangars, trees, or other structures can create unexpected downdrafts or wind shear on final approach. The intensity is proportional to wind speed and surface roughness.
exam_tip: Mechanical turbulence: wind over surface obstacles creates eddies and wake. Proportional to wind speed and roughness. Hazard on approach near hangars/trees.
key_facts: Wind flowing over surface obstacles creates eddies
Proportional to wind speed and surface roughness
Strongest in strong winds over rough terrain
Low-level hazard on approach near obstacles
Hangars, trees, hills create wake turbulence on final approach
Rotor zones behind ridges are extreme mechanical turbulence

syllabus_desc: Describe the four main formation mechanisms for turbulence: clear air turbulence (CAT).
concept: Clear Air Turbulence (CAT) occurs in cloud-free air, most commonly at high altitudes (FL250–FL450) in or near jet streams where strong vertical wind shear exists. It cannot be detected visually, by weather radar, or by standard instruments. It is caused by Kelvin-Helmholtz instabilities at wind shear boundaries. CAT is most severe: within and near jet stream cores, on the equatorward (cold) side of the jet, in areas of high-amplitude upper-level waves, near the tropopause, and in mountain wave systems. Intensity ranges from light to extreme. PIREPs are the primary source of CAT information.
exam_tip: CAT: turbulence in clear air (no cloud), usually near jet streams at FL250–450. Not radar-detectable. Caused by wind shear. Reported by PIREPs.
key_facts: Clear Air Turbulence: no cloud, not visually detectable
Most common at FL250–FL450 near jet streams
Caused by Kelvin-Helmholtz instability in wind shear zones
Cannot be detected by weather radar
Most severe on the equatorward side of jet stream core
Also in mountain wave systems
PIREPs are the primary detection/reporting tool

syllabus_desc: Describe the four main formation mechanisms for turbulence: wake turbulence.
concept: Wake turbulence is generated by any aircraft in flight as a byproduct of lift production — the wingtip vortices. Two counter-rotating vortices trail behind the aircraft, each rotating inward at the top (the left vortex rotates anticlockwise, the right vortex clockwise when viewed from behind). Wake turbulence intensity is proportional to: aircraft weight (heavier = stronger vortices), wing span (shorter = stronger), speed (slower = stronger). Vortices sink at ~300–500 ft/min and drift downwind. In calm conditions they can persist for several minutes. Most hazardous for light aircraft following heavy aircraft on approach in calm, light-wind conditions.
exam_tip: Wake turbulence: wingtip vortices from lift generation. Heavier/slower/shorter wing = stronger. Vortices sink and drift downwind. Most hazardous light behind heavy on approach.
key_facts: Generated by lift production — wingtip vortices
Counter-rotating vortices trail behind aircraft
Intensity proportional to: weight (heavier = stronger)
Also: slower speed and shorter wingspan = stronger vortices
Vortices sink at ~300–500 ft/min
Drift downwind in light winds
Persist several minutes in calm conditions
Most hazardous: light aircraft following heavy on approach in calm wind

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the following enhancing factors on 20.40.2 (a), (b) and (c) from: atmospheric stability.
concept: Atmospheric stability affects convective (a), mechanical (b), and CAT (c) turbulence: Instability enhances convective turbulence by allowing thermals to continue rising higher and with greater vigour. For mechanical turbulence, instability allows eddies to grow vertically and persist longer. For CAT, stability at jet stream altitudes affects the shear zone — a stable layer immediately adjacent to a wind shear zone can develop KH instabilities and CAT more readily when disturbed. Stability suppresses convective and mechanical turbulence by limiting the vertical extent of eddies.
exam_tip: Instability enhances turbulence (a, b, c): convective thermals stronger, mechanical eddies grow higher, KH instabilities develop more readily. Stability suppresses turbulence.
key_facts: Instability: enhances convective turbulence (deeper, more vigorous thermals)
Instability: allows mechanical eddies to grow vertically
Stable layers adjacent to shear zones: can develop KH instabilities
Stability: suppresses convective and mechanical turbulence
Stability: limits vertical extent of eddies and thermals

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the following enhancing factors on 20.40.2 (a), (b) and (c) from: surface roughness.
concept: Surface roughness enhances mechanical turbulence (b) and convective turbulence (a) near the surface. Rough surfaces (mountains, forests, cities) create more flow separation and eddies for mechanical turbulence. Rough surfaces also absorb more solar energy (lower albedo) and have more varied heating, enhancing thermals and convective turbulence. Rough terrain enhances orographic effects and mountain wave development. Smooth surfaces (ocean, flat plains) produce less turbulence of both types.
exam_tip: Rough surface: enhances mechanical turbulence (more eddies) and convective turbulence (varied heating). Mountains → strongest effect. Smooth sea → least turbulence.
key_facts: Rough surfaces create more flow separation → mechanical eddies
Mountains/forests/cities = highest surface roughness turbulence
Rough surfaces have varied heating → enhanced thermals
Smooth surfaces (ocean, plains) produce less turbulence
Also enhances orographic uplift and mountain wave

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the following enhancing factors on 20.40.2 (a), (b) and (c) from: wind speed/direction.
concept: Higher wind speed increases mechanical turbulence intensity (proportional to wind speed squared). Strong winds enhance wind shear and hence CAT potential near jet streams and fronts. Wind direction relative to terrain determines the extent of orographic effects: flow perpendicular to a ridge maximises mountain wave and mechanical turbulence. Strong low-level wind shear (veering or backing with altitude) destabilises the air and generates turbulence at the shear interface. Sudden direction changes can create vortices and turbulence at the shear boundary.
exam_tip: Higher wind speed: mechanical turbulence ∝ V². Flow perpendicular to ridge: maximum orographic/wave turbulence. Strong shear → CAT and KH instabilities.
key_facts: Mechanical turbulence intensity proportional to wind speed squared
Strong wind enhances wind shear → more CAT potential
Flow perpendicular to ridge = maximum mountain wave and turbulence
Directional wind shear (veering/backing) destabilises air
Sudden direction changes create turbulent shear zones

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the following enhancing factors on 20.40.2 (a), (b) and (c) from: vertical wind-shear.
concept: Vertical wind shear (change of wind speed and/or direction with altitude) is the primary driver of CAT (c) and significantly enhances mechanical turbulence (b). Strong vertical shear in the jet stream region creates KH instabilities at shear boundaries. Low-level wind shear (change of wind with altitude in the lowest few thousand feet) creates dangerous approach and departure conditions. The Richardson Number (Ri) quantifies the relative roles of stability and shear — when Ri < 0.25, KH instabilities develop and turbulence is expected. Strong vertical shear combined with low stability always produces turbulence.
exam_tip: Vertical wind shear: primary driver of CAT. Low-level shear = approach hazard. Richardson Number Ri < 0.25 → KH instabilities → turbulence.
key_facts: Vertical wind shear = primary driver of CAT
Strong shear in jet stream region → Kelvin-Helmholtz instabilities
Low-level wind shear: dangerous on approach and departure
Richardson Number Ri = stability/shear ratio
Ri < 0.25 → KH instabilities → turbulence expected
Strong shear + low stability = turbulence guaranteed

topic: Jet Stream — Overview and Aviation Effects
syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: surface friction.
concept: Surface friction causes wind speed to increase with height in the friction layer (boundary layer). This creates a vertical wind shear — the wind near the surface is slower and deflected toward low pressure compared to the geostrophic wind above. The shear is greatest where: surface roughness is high (forests, cities), wind speed is strong, and the atmosphere is stable (deep inversion suppresses mixing). The resulting low-level wind shear can cause significant airspeed changes on approach and departure, particularly if the aircraft transitions between the slow surface layer and the faster wind above.
exam_tip: Surface friction: wind increases with altitude in boundary layer → vertical shear. Greatest with rough surface, strong wind, stable atmosphere. Airspeed change on approach/departure.
key_facts: Wind speed increases with altitude in the friction layer
Slower wind near surface due to friction drag
Deflected wind near surface versus geostrophic wind above
Greatest shear: rough surface + strong wind + stable atmosphere
Airspeed change on approach as aircraft enters/exits friction layer
Largest effect on approach in stable high-pressure conditions

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: thunderstorms.
concept: Thunderstorms produce intense low-level wind shear through: (1) Microbursts — concentrated downdraft diverging at the surface, creating sudden headwind/downdraft/tailwind sequence. (2) Gust front — cold outflow air radiating outward ahead of the storm, producing sudden wind shifts. (3) Downdraft inflow — strong inflow at low level into the downdraft creates violent wind shear near the storm base. All forms of thunderstorm wind shear are most hazardous during takeoff and approach at low altitudes where aircraft speed is low and height margin is small.
exam_tip: Thunderstorm shear: microburst (headwind → downdraft → tailwind), gust front (sudden wind shift), downdraft inflow. All catastrophic on takeoff/approach at low altitude.
key_facts: Microburst: intense downdraft → headwind then tailwind sequence
Gust front: cold outflow radiates ahead of storm
Sudden wind shifts in gust front (up to 180°)
Downdraft inflow: strong wind shear near storm base
Most hazardous on takeoff and approach at low altitude
Microburst: most dangerous — 50+ kt airspeed change possible

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: temperature inversions.
concept: A temperature inversion traps a different wind regime below it compared to above. The stable inversion layer acts as a lid, preventing vertical mixing between the two layers. Above the inversion: winds may be strong and close to geostrophic. Below the inversion: light, variable winds in the cold pool. The transition at the inversion base is a zone of strong vertical wind shear — aircraft descending through the inversion encounter a sudden change in wind speed and direction. This wind shear is particularly hazardous if encountered on approach in the presence of a radiation or subsidence inversion.
exam_tip: Inversion: different wind regimes above and below. Strong vertical shear AT the inversion base. Hazard on approach: sudden wind change descending through inversion.
key_facts: Temperature inversion separates two wind regimes
Above inversion: stronger winds near geostrophic
Below inversion: light variable winds in cold pool
Strong vertical shear at the inversion base
Aircraft descending through inversion encounters sudden wind change
Most hazardous on approach in radiation/subsidence inversion

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: frontal activity.
concept: Fronts mark the boundary between two air masses with different temperatures, pressures, and wind regimes. Significant wind shear exists at the frontal surface: above the front the wind may be geostrophic; below it the surface wind in the cold air may be very different in speed and direction. Cold fronts produce the most intense low-level shear — the frontal passage brings a sudden wind veer, speed change, and gust. The zone of most intense shear is in the lowest 5,000 ft. Aircraft on approach or departure through a front may experience sudden large airspeed changes and attitude upsets.
exam_tip: Frontal shear: different wind regimes in warm vs cold air. Cold fronts: sudden veer, speed change, gust on passage. Most intense shear in lowest 5,000 ft.
key_facts: Different wind regimes exist in warm and cold air masses
Wind shear zone at and near the frontal surface
Cold front: most intense shear — sudden veer and speed change
Gust on frontal passage
Most intense shear in lowest 5,000 ft
Hazard on approach/departure if front is near the aerodrome

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: wake turbulence from fixed and rotary winged aircraft.
concept: Wake vortices from aircraft (and rotor downwash from helicopters) create intense low-level wind shear in the vicinity of an aerodrome. Fixed-wing vortices: counter-rotating wingtip vortices descend and drift downwind; persist minutes in calm conditions; encounter by a following aircraft causes sudden roll and airspeed change. Rotary-wing (helicopter) downwash: intense circular downwash, particularly dangerous for light aircraft during approach/departure near helicopters. Most hazardous: light aircraft following heavy, calm winds, low aircraft altitude on final approach.
exam_tip: Wake vortex shear: fixed-wing wingtip vortices and helicopter downwash. Most hazardous: light following heavy on calm-day approach. Vortices sink, drift downwind.
key_facts: Fixed-wing: counter-rotating wingtip vortices descend and drift
Persist for minutes in calm conditions
Encounter causes sudden roll and airspeed change
Most hazardous: light following heavy in calm conditions on approach
Rotary-wing: intense circular downwash from helicopters
Dangerous for light aircraft near helicopters on the ground/approach

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques used to avoid or minimize the effects of low-level wind-shear.
concept: Techniques: (1) Pre-flight: check PIREPs, TAFs, wind forecasts, SIGMETs for wind shear warnings (LLWS/LWAS reports). (2) Awareness of conditions that produce wind shear (thunderstorms, fronts, inversions). (3) Use higher approach/departure speeds — more kinetic energy provides margin. (4) Increase threshold crossings speeds in known shear conditions. (5) On approach: monitor airspeed closely; if airspeed drops below target by more than 15 kts or rate of descent becomes excessive, execute immediate go-around. (6) Avoid airports with known active wind shear hazards. (7) Follow ATC wind shear reports and PIREPs.
exam_tip: Avoid/minimise: pre-flight check PIREPs/SIGMET, higher approach speed, monitor airspeed on approach (go-around if >15 kt loss), follow LWAS/LLWS reports.
key_facts: Pre-flight: check PIREPs, SIGMETs, LLWS/LWAS reports
Increase approach and departure speeds in wind shear conditions
Monitor airspeed closely on approach
Go-around immediately if airspeed drops >15 kts below target
Go-around if rate of descent becomes excessive
Follow ATC wind shear reports and PIREPs
Avoid flying in vicinity of active thunderstorms

syllabus_desc: Describe, in accordance with the ICAO definitions, the characteristics of: light turbulence.
concept: ICAO light turbulence: aircraft experiences slight, erratic changes in altitude and/or attitude. Occupants may feel slight strain against seatbelts. Unsecured objects may be displaced slightly. Food service and walking are possible. Estimated intensity: +/−15 ft/s vertical gust, +/−0.2 to 0.5g. Reported in PIREPs as "LGT TURB". Does not affect normal aircraft performance significantly.
exam_tip: ICAO Light turbulence: slight erratic altitude/attitude changes. Seatbelts feel slight strain. Occupants can walk. ±0.2–0.5g. Reported: LGT TURB.
key_facts: Slight, erratic changes in altitude and/or attitude
Occupants feel slight strain against seatbelts
Unsecured objects may shift slightly
Walking and food service possible
Estimated: ±0.2–0.5g vertical acceleration
PIREP code: LGT TURB

syllabus_desc: Describe, in accordance with the ICAO definitions, the characteristics of: moderate turbulence.
concept: ICAO moderate turbulence: similar to light turbulence but of greater intensity. Changes in altitude and/or attitude occur but aircraft remains in positive control at all times. Occupants feel definite strains against seatbelts. Unsecured objects are dislodged. Food service and walking are difficult. Estimated intensity: +/−15 to 30 ft/s vertical gust, +/−0.5 to 1.0g. Reported as "MOD TURB". Aircraft performance is noticeably affected but still controllable.
exam_tip: ICAO Moderate turbulence: definite strain on seatbelts, objects dislodged, difficult walking. ±0.5–1.0g. Aircraft controllable. Reported: MOD TURB.
key_facts: Similar to light but greater intensity
Aircraft remains in positive control
Definite strain on seatbelts
Unsecured objects dislodged
Walking and food service difficult
Estimated: ±0.5–1.0g vertical acceleration
PIREP code: MOD TURB

syllabus_desc: Describe, in accordance with the ICAO definitions, the characteristics of: severe turbulence.
concept: ICAO severe turbulence: large, abrupt changes in altitude and/or attitude; aircraft may be momentarily out of control. Occupants are forced violently against seatbelts. Unsecured objects are tossed about. Food service and walking are impossible. Estimated intensity: >30 ft/s vertical gust, >1.0g. Reported as "SEV TURB". Structural damage is possible. Airspeed must be immediately reduced to the turbulence penetration speed (Va). Extreme turbulence is even greater — structural damage is likely.
exam_tip: ICAO Severe turbulence: large abrupt changes, momentarily out of control, >1.0g, objects thrown violently. Structural damage possible. Reduce to Va immediately.
key_facts: Large abrupt changes in altitude and attitude
Aircraft may be momentarily out of control
Occupants forced violently against seatbelts
Unsecured objects thrown violently
Estimated: >1.0g vertical acceleration
Structural damage possible
Reduce airspeed to Va (turbulence penetration speed) immediately
PIREP code: SEV TURB

syllabus_desc: Explain the methods by which the aviation community is advised of volcanic eruptions within the New Zealand FIR.
concept: New Zealand volcanic ash advisories are coordinated through: (1) Volcanic Ash Advisory Centre (VAAC) Wellington - issues Volcanic Ash Advisories (VAAs) for the NZ FIR and South Pacific/Southern Ocean. (2) SIGMET - meteorological watch offices issue volcanic ash SIGMETs for the affected airspace. (3) NOTAM - issued by Airways NZ for volcanic ash affecting NZ aerodromes or airspace. (4) GNS Science / GeoNet - monitors NZ volcanoes (Ruapehu, Tongariro, White Island/Whakaari) and advises CAA. (5) PIREPs - pilots report volcanic ash encounters. NZ has several active volcanoes making this a significant aviation risk.
exam_tip: NZ volcanic ash: VAAC Wellington issues VAAs. SIGMETs from Met watch office. NOTAMs from Airways NZ. GNS Science/GeoNet monitors NZ volcanoes.
key_facts: VAAC Wellington: issues Volcanic Ash Advisories (VAAs)
SIGMETs: issued by meteorological watch office for ash in airspace
NOTAMs: issued by Airways NZ for affected aerodromes/airspace
GNS Science / GeoNet: monitors NZ active volcanoes
PIREPs: pilots report ash encounters
NZ active volcanoes: Ruapehu, Tongariro, White Island (Whakaari)

syllabus_desc: Explain the hazards to aviation of volcanic ash encountered: in flight.
concept: Volcanic ash in flight hazards: (1) Engine damage/flameout - ash melts in turbine stages (above 1,000 C) and re-solidifies as glass on turbine blades, causing engine flameout. (2) Abrasion of windscreen - ash erodes windscreen to opaque state. (3) Contamination of pitot/static ports - erroneous instrument readings. (4) Damage to airframe surfaces - sandblasting of paintwork, erosion of leading edges. (5) Blocked cabin air filters. (6) Contaminated fuel system. (7) Sulphur dioxide odour inside cabin. Ash clouds may not be visible at night or in IMC. Multiple engine flameouts at altitude have occurred historically.
exam_tip: In-flight ash: engine flameout (ash melts then re-solidifies on turbine blades), abraded windscreen, blocked pitot/static, airframe erosion. Ash invisible at night. AVOID.
key_facts: Engine flameout: ash melts (>1,000C) and re-solidifies on turbine blades
Windscreen abraded to opaque state
Pitot/static ports contaminated - false instruments
Leading edge and paintwork erosion
Sulphur dioxide fumes in cabin
Ash clouds invisible at night or in IMC
Multiple engine flameouts have occurred historically

topic: Snow — Aviation Visibility and Surface Hazards
syllabus_desc: Explain the hazards to aviation of volcanic ash encountered: during the take-off and landing phases on an ash contaminated runway.
concept: Volcanic ash on runways and aerodromes: (1) Reduced braking effectiveness - ash acts as lubricant when wet. (2) Engine ingestion during ground operations - ground vortices can ingest surface ash deposits. (3) Pitot/static contamination from ash disturbed by prop/jet wash. (4) Contaminated fuel - ash deposited in fuel tanks during refuelling. (5) Reduced visibility in blowing ash. (6) Ash accumulation on aircraft surfaces - weight increase on parked aircraft. (7) Runway friction testing required before operations. Runways must be swept before resuming operations after significant ashfall.
exam_tip: Ash on runway: reduced braking (ash = lubricant when wet), engine ingestion, pitot contamination, reduced visibility in blowing ash. Runway must be swept before operations.
key_facts: Reduced braking: ash acts as lubricant when wet
Engine ash ingestion during ground operations (ground vortices)
Pitot/static contaminated by prop/jet blast disturbing surface ash
Reduced visibility in blowing ash during taxi/takeoff/landing
Weight accumulation on parked aircraft
Runways must be swept before operations after ashfall
Runway friction testing required

syllabus_desc: Explain the development of, and the hazards associated with, flight in the following conditions: duststorms.
concept: Duststorms develop when strong winds over dry, bare terrain lift large quantities of dust particles into the atmosphere. They form in arid or semi-arid regions when a strong pressure gradient or frontal system drives gusty winds over dry soil. They can extend thousands of metres altitude and hundreds of kilometres horizontally. Aviation hazards: sudden reduction of visibility to zero, abrasion of airframe and windscreen, engine and filter contamination, electrostatic charge build-up (static discharge), instrument contamination. Duststorms can arrive rapidly with little warning. In NZ context, dust haze can occur in dry parts of Canterbury/Otago in strong northwest winds.
exam_tip: Duststorm: strong winds over dry terrain lift dust. Sudden zero visibility, engine contamination, airframe abrasion, static. Rapid onset with little warning.
key_facts: Strong winds over dry bare terrain lift dust particles
Can extend to thousands of metres altitude
Sudden reduction of visibility to zero
Airframe and windscreen abrasion
Engine and filter contamination
Electrostatic charge build-up
Rapid onset with little warning

syllabus_desc: Explain the development of, and the hazards associated with, flight in the following conditions: blowing surface snow (blizzards).
concept: Blizzards involve strong winds (>30 kts) and heavy snow with visibility reduced to less than 400 m. They develop in cold conditions when a vigorous low-pressure system drives strong winds over snow-covered or snowfall areas. Aviation hazards: zero or near-zero visibility, airframe icing (mixed precipitation with supercooled content), severe turbulence in gusty wind, whiteout conditions (loss of horizon and depth perception over snow-covered terrain), control surface icing, engine icing, runway contamination making takeoff and landing hazardous.
exam_tip: Blizzard: strong wind (>30 kt) + heavy snow → near-zero visibility. Whiteout, severe icing, severe turbulence, contaminated runway.
key_facts: Strong winds >30 kts with heavy snow
Visibility below 400 m
Severe icing potential
Whiteout: loss of horizon over snow (visual illusion)
Severe turbulence in gusty blizzard conditions
Engine and control surface icing
Runway contamination: very hazardous operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the development of, and the hazards associated with, flight in the following conditions: whiteout (visual illusion type).
concept: Whiteout is a visual illusion that occurs when flying over a uniformly white snow-covered surface under an overcast sky of similar reflectance. The horizon disappears and the pilot loses depth perception and height reference. It is distinct from blizzard whiteout (which is weather-induced). Whiteout illusion: the sky, horizon, and snow surface blend into a single white field of view; the pilot cannot distinguish the surface from the sky; terrain features become invisible; controlled flight into terrain is the primary risk. It typically occurs in polar/alpine regions in overcast conditions and is most dangerous at low altitude.
exam_tip: Whiteout (visual illusion): uniform white snow + overcast sky → horizon disappears. Cannot distinguish terrain from sky. CFIT risk. Overcast + snow-covered terrain.
key_facts: Uniform white snow surface + overcast sky of similar reflectance
