Horizon disappears - sky and snow blend together
Loss of depth perception and height reference
Cannot distinguish terrain features or surface
Controlled Flight Into Terrain (CFIT) primary risk
Occurs in polar/alpine regions in overcast conditions
Most dangerous at low altitude and low airspeed

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by 'the general circulation'.
concept: The general circulation refers to the large-scale, global pattern of atmospheric motion driven by the unequal heating of the Earth's surface (more solar energy received at the equator than at the poles). The general circulation redistributes heat from the equator to the poles and drives the world's persistent wind systems, pressure belts, and weather patterns. It is characterised by three cells in each hemisphere: the Hadley cell (equatorial-subtropical), the Ferrel cell (subtropical-subpolar), and the Polar cell. The general circulation explains the subtropical high-pressure belts, trade winds, mid-latitude westerlies, and polar easterlies.
exam_tip: General circulation: global atmospheric motion driven by unequal heating (equator hot, poles cold). Three cells each hemisphere: Hadley, Ferrel, Polar. Drives trade winds, westerlies, polar easterlies.
key_facts: Global pattern of atmospheric motion
Driven by unequal heating: equator receives more solar energy than poles
Redistributes heat from equator to poles
Three cells per hemisphere: Hadley, Ferrel, Polar
Hadley cell: equatorial to subtropical
Ferrel cell: subtropical to subpolar
Polar cell: subpolar to pole
Explains persistent global wind belts and pressure systems

syllabus_desc: Based on a diagram of the 'general circulation', explain why the following global weather features exist: polar highs.
concept: Polar highs exist because the polar regions receive very little solar radiation, causing intense surface cooling. Cold dense air accumulates at the poles, creating a permanent or semi-permanent high-pressure region (polar high). The polar high is a thermal cold high — air is dense due to very low temperatures. In the Southern Hemisphere, the Antarctic ice sheet creates one of the most intense and permanent high-pressure systems on Earth. Cold air flows outward from the polar high as polar easterlies.
exam_tip: Polar highs: intense surface cooling at poles creates cold dense air and surface high pressure. Antarctic high is strongest and most permanent. Feeds polar easterlies.
key_facts: Polar regions: very little solar radiation received
Intense surface cooling creates cold, dense air
Cold dense air creates surface high pressure
Thermal cold high — density driven
SH: Antarctic ice sheet creates intense permanent high
Cold air flows outward as polar easterlies

syllabus_desc: Based on a diagram of the 'general circulation', explain why the following global weather features exist: polar easterlies.
concept: Polar easterlies flow outward from the polar high toward lower latitudes. As cold dense air flows away from the pole, the Coriolis force deflects it. In the Southern Hemisphere, outward-flowing air is deflected to the left, creating winds from the east (easterlies) in polar regions. They are generally cold, dry, and light to moderate in the lowest levels. In the SH, polar easterlies are found south of approximately 60-65 degrees South.
exam_tip: Polar easterlies: cold air flows outward from polar high; Coriolis deflects it to the LEFT in SH creating easterly winds. Found south of ~60-65 degrees S.
key_facts: Cold air flows outward from polar high
Coriolis deflects outward flow to the left in SH
Results in winds from the east (easterlies)
Generally cold, dry, light to moderate
Found south of approximately 60-65 degrees S in the SH
Connect the polar cell to the subpolar low-pressure trough

syllabus_desc: Based on a diagram of the 'general circulation', explain why the following global weather features exist: polar lows.
concept: Polar lows are small but intense low-pressure systems that develop over polar oceans during cold air outbreaks. They form when cold polar air flows out over relatively warm ocean surfaces, destabilising the lower atmosphere and triggering intense convection. They are most common in the Norwegian Sea and Southern Ocean. In the context of the general circulation, the subpolar low-pressure belt (around 60 degrees latitude) is the zone where the polar cell and Ferrel cell meet, and where cold polar air meets warmer mid-latitude air, creating the polar front and frequent cyclogenesis.
exam_tip: Polar lows: intense small lows over polar oceans when cold air overruns warm ocean. Subpolar low belt at ~60 degrees: where polar and Ferrel cells meet, frequent cyclogenesis.
key_facts: Polar lows: small intense lows, cold air over warm polar ocean
Subpolar low-pressure belt at approximately 60 degrees latitude
Zone where polar and Ferrel cells converge
Cold polar air meets warmer mid-latitude air (polar front)
Frequent cyclogenesis in this zone
Most active: Norwegian Sea and Southern Ocean

syllabus_desc: Based on a diagram of the 'general circulation', explain why the following global weather features exist: polar fronts.
concept: The polar front is the boundary between cold polar air (from the polar/Ferrel cell) and warmer mid-latitude air (from the Ferrel cell). It is the birthplace of mid-latitude depressions. The polar front is discontinuous and variable in position but generally found around 45-65 degrees latitude. Jet streams associated with the polar front (polar front jet stream) mark the zone of maximum temperature contrast. The polar front migrates equatorward in winter and poleward in summer, following the seasonal shift of temperature gradients.
exam_tip: Polar front: boundary between cold polar air and warm mid-latitude air. Birthplace of mid-latitude depressions. Polar jet stream above it. Position 45-65 degrees, varies seasonally.
key_facts: Boundary between cold polar and warm mid-latitude air
Birthplace of mid-latitude (extratropical) depressions
Found approximately 45-65 degrees latitude
Variable and discontinuous in position
Polar front jet stream flows above the polar front
Migrates equatorward in winter, poleward in summer

syllabus_desc: Based on a diagram of the 'general circulation', explain why the following global weather features exist: mid-latitude westerlies.
concept: Mid-latitude westerlies (the prevailing westerlies) exist because the Ferrel cell drives surface air from subtropical highs (around 30 degrees) toward the subpolar lows (around 60 degrees). As this air flows poleward, Coriolis deflects it to the left in the SH, creating westerly winds (from the west). The westerlies dominate the mid-latitudes (30-60 degrees S in the SH), driving the roaring forties, furious fifties, and screaming sixties. They steer mid-latitude depressions from west to east across NZ.
exam_tip: Mid-lat westerlies: Ferrel cell drives air poleward from subtropical highs; Coriolis deflects left (SH) = westerly. Roaring 40s/50s/60s. Steers depressions E across NZ.
key_facts: Ferrel cell drives air from subtropical high toward subpolar low
Coriolis deflects poleward flow to the left in SH
Results in winds from the west (westerlies)
Dominate mid-latitudes: 30-60 degrees S in SH
Roaring Forties, Furious Fifties, Screaming Sixties
Steer mid-latitude depressions eastward across New Zealand

syllabus_desc: Based on a diagram of the 'general circulation', explain why the following global weather features exist: mid-latitude anticyclones.
concept: Mid-latitude anticyclones (blocking highs) form when upper-level Rossby wave patterns create areas of upper convergence that force air downward, building surface high pressure. They block the normal eastward progression of depressions and can persist for days to weeks. They are distinct from the semi-permanent subtropical highs and represent the large-scale ridges that dominate mid-latitude weather patterns periodically. In the SH, mid-latitude highs migrating eastward across NZ bring settled, fine weather between depression passages.
exam_tip: Mid-lat anticyclones: upper-level Rossby wave convergence forces air down = surface high. Blocking highs disrupt normal depression tracks. NZ: bring settled weather between lows.
key_facts: Form from upper-level convergence in Rossby wave patterns
Block normal eastward progression of depressions
Can persist days to weeks
Distinct from semi-permanent subtropical highs
SH: migrate eastward across NZ between depressions
Bring settled, fine weather to NZ

syllabus_desc: Based on a diagram of the 'general circulation', explain why the following global weather features exist: sub-tropical trade winds.
concept: The sub-tropical trade winds (SE trade winds in SH, NE in NH) blow from the subtropical high-pressure belts toward the equatorial trough (ITCZ). Air subsiding in the Hadley cell creates the subtropical high at the surface. This air flows equatorward toward low pressure; Coriolis deflects it to the left in the SH, creating SE winds (the SE trade winds). They are the most consistent winds on Earth, blowing steadily year-round in the tropics and subtropics. They converge at the ITCZ where they rise, completing the Hadley cell.
exam_tip: SE trade winds (SH): air flows from subtropical high toward equatorial low; Coriolis deflects left = SE direction. Most consistent winds on Earth. Converge at ITCZ.
key_facts: Hadley cell subsidence creates subtropical high at surface
Surface air flows equatorward toward ITCZ
Coriolis deflects equatorward flow to left in SH = SE winds
SE trade winds in Southern Hemisphere; NE in Northern
Most consistent winds on Earth (year-round)

topic: Upper Air Patterns and Surface Weather
syllabus_desc: Based on a diagram of the 'general circulation', explain why the following global weather features exist: the equatorial trough.
concept: The equatorial trough (also called the Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone, ITCZ) is a belt of low pressure near the equator where the SE trade winds (SH) and NE trade winds (NH) converge. The intense solar heating at the equator causes deep convection and rising air, creating low surface pressure. The convergence of the trade winds provides additional lifting. The equatorial trough is characterised by light variable winds (the doldrums), heavy convective precipitation, deep cumulonimbus, and high humidity. It migrates seasonally following the sun.
exam_tip: Equatorial trough/ITCZ: convergence of SE and NE trade winds at equator. Intense solar heating, deep convection, low pressure, doldrums, heavy rain/Cb. Migrates seasonally.
key_facts: Belt of low pressure near the equator
Trade winds from both hemispheres converge here
Intense solar heating drives deep convection
Light variable winds (doldrums) near the convergence
Heavy convective precipitation and deep Cb
Migrates seasonally: follows the sun N/S
Called ITCZ (Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone)

syllabus_desc: State the approximate latitude limits of the tropics.
concept: The tropics are defined astronomically by the Tropics of Cancer (23.5 degrees N) and Capricorn (23.5 degrees S) - the latitudes where the sun is directly overhead at the solstices. For meteorological purposes, the tropical zone is generally considered to extend to approximately 30 degrees in each hemisphere, encompassing the trade wind belt and the equatorial trough. New Zealand is in the subtropical zone (34-46 degrees S) - outside the tropics but influenced by tropical systems (tropical cyclones, trade winds, ITCZ/SPCZ).
exam_tip: Tropics: Cancer (23.5 N) to Capricorn (23.5 S). Meteorologically to ~30 degrees each hemisphere. NZ (34-46 S) = subtropical - outside tropics but influenced by them.
key_facts: Tropic of Cancer: 23.5 degrees North
Tropic of Capricorn: 23.5 degrees South
Meteorological tropics: approximately 0-30 degrees each hemisphere
New Zealand: 34-46 degrees S = subtropical
NZ influenced by tropical systems (cyclones, trade winds, SPCZ)
Sun is overhead at tropics during respective solstice

syllabus_desc: Describe the 'Hadley cell'.
concept: The Hadley cell is a large-scale atmospheric circulation cell in each hemisphere, driven by solar heating at the equator. It operates as follows: (1) Intense solar heating at the equator causes air to rise (creating the equatorial trough/ITCZ). (2) The rising air flows poleward at high altitude in both hemispheres. (3) At approximately 25-30 degrees latitude the air has cooled sufficiently to subside, creating the subtropical high-pressure belt. (4) At the surface, air flows back toward the equator as the trade winds. The Hadley cell is the dominant tropical circulation and is directly responsible for trade winds, subtropical highs, and equatorial convection.
exam_tip: Hadley cell: rises at equator (ITCZ), flows poleward at altitude, subsides at ~25-30 degrees (subtropical high), returns to equator at surface as trade winds.
key_facts: Rising air at the equator (intense solar heating, ITCZ)
High-altitude poleward flow
Subsidence at approximately 25-30 degrees latitude
Creates subtropical high-pressure belt
Surface return flow toward equator = trade winds
Dominant circulation in the tropics
Responsible for trade winds, subtropical highs, equatorial convection

syllabus_desc: Describe what is meant by: horse latitudes.
concept: The horse latitudes are the subtropical regions (approximately 25-35 degrees N and S) where the Hadley cell produces subsiding air and the subtropical high-pressure belt. They are characterised by light, variable winds, warm temperatures, little precipitation, and clear skies. The name originates from historical accounts of sailing ships becalmed in these latitudes, where horses being transported died from heat and lack of water, and were thrown overboard. The horse latitudes correspond to the semi-permanent subtropical high-pressure systems: the South Pacific High, South Atlantic High, and South Indian Ocean High.
exam_tip: Horse latitudes: ~25-35 degrees, subtropical high belt, Hadley cell subsidence. Light variable winds (calm), warm, dry, little rain. Semi-permanent subtropical highs.
key_facts: Approximately 25-35 degrees N and S latitude
Zone of Hadley cell subsidence
Subtropical high-pressure belt
Light, variable winds - near calm conditions
Warm, dry, little precipitation
Name origin: sailing ships becalmed, horses thrown overboard
Semi-permanent highs: South Pacific, South Atlantic, South Indian

syllabus_desc: Describe what is meant by: doldrums.
concept: The doldrums are the equatorial zone (approximately 5 degrees N to 5 degrees S, but variable) characterised by light, variable winds and calm conditions associated with the equatorial trough (ITCZ). The trade winds from both hemispheres converge in the doldrums, producing intense convective weather (deep cumulonimbus, heavy rain, thunderstorms) but near-calm surface winds. Sailing ships were historically becalmed in the doldrums for extended periods. The doldrums migrate seasonally with the ITCZ. Aviation significance: extreme convective weather despite near-calm surface winds; difficult to forecast exact position.
exam_tip: Doldrums: equatorial zone (~5 N to 5 S), light/calm surface winds, but intense Cb and heavy rain. ITCZ location. Trade winds converge here. Migrate seasonally.
key_facts: Equatorial zone: approximately 5 degrees N to 5 degrees S
Light, variable, near-calm surface winds
Intense convective weather: deep Cb, heavy rain, thunderstorms
Trade winds from both hemispheres converge here
Sailing ships historically becalmed here
Migrate seasonally with ITCZ
Calm winds at surface despite violent convection above

syllabus_desc: Describe the equatorial trough and the inter-tropical convergence zone (ITCZ).
concept: The equatorial trough is a belt of low surface pressure near the equator, produced by intense solar heating and the convergence of trade winds from both hemispheres. The ITCZ (Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone) is the meteorological term for the active portion of the equatorial trough where deep convection and convergence are most pronounced. Characteristics: light surface winds (doldrums), deep convective cloud systems (Cb), heavy rainfall and thunderstorms, high humidity, active from the surface to the tropopause. The ITCZ migrates northward (NH summer) and southward (SH summer) following maximum solar heating. It is most active and further poleward over land.
exam_tip: ITCZ: active convergence zone of the equatorial trough. Deep Cb, heavy rain, light surface winds, high humidity. Migrates seasonally N (NH summer) and S (SH summer).
key_facts: Belt of low pressure near the equator
Convergence of trade winds from both hemispheres
Deep convective cloud (Cb) and heavy rainfall
Light variable surface winds (doldrums)
High humidity and temperatures
Migrates seasonally following maximum solar heating
Further from equator over land than over ocean
Most active precipitation zone in the tropics

syllabus_desc: State the: seasonal location of the equatorial trough and ITCZ.
concept: The ITCZ migrates seasonally following the sun: June-August (NH summer) - ITCZ moves north, reaching approximately 10-15 degrees N; farthest north over land in Asia and Africa. December-February (SH summer) - ITCZ moves south, reaching approximately 5-10 degrees S; more pronounced southern displacement over land in South America and Africa. Near-equatorial position year-round over the ocean. The SPCZ (South Pacific Convergence Zone) is related to the ITCZ and extends SE from the Coral Sea toward French Polynesia, shifting NE in SH winter and SW in SH summer.
exam_tip: ITCZ: NH summer (Jun-Aug) = moves north to ~10-15 N. SH summer (Dec-Feb) = moves south to ~5-10 S. More displacement over land than ocean. SPCZ related feature.
key_facts: June-August: ITCZ moves north to ~10-15 degrees N
December-February: ITCZ moves south to ~5-10 degrees S
Greater seasonal migration over land than ocean
Over ocean: near-equatorial position year-round
Follows maximum solar heating (overhead sun)
SPCZ in SH is related to ITCZ - shifts seasonally too

syllabus_desc: State the: typical low and mid-level weather in an active and inactive ITCZ.
concept: Active ITCZ: deep cumulonimbus to the tropopause, organised clusters of Cb (MCS), heavy rain and thunderstorms, squall lines, near-surface convergence and strong updrafts, visibility poor in rain. Inactive ITCZ: shallow convection only (Cu to 5,000-10,000 ft), little precipitation, mostly stratocumulus or fair weather cumulus, light winds, occasional brief showers. The ITCZ can alternate between active and inactive phases over days and weeks, related to synoptic-scale waves in the easterlies.
exam_tip: Active ITCZ: deep Cb to tropopause, heavy rain, organised MCS, poor visibility. Inactive ITCZ: shallow Cu, little rain, light winds, occasional brief showers.
key_facts: Active ITCZ: deep Cb to tropopause
Active: organised convective systems (MCS), squall lines
Active: heavy rain, thunderstorms, poor visibility
Active: strong convergence and updrafts near surface
Inactive ITCZ: shallow cumulus only, 5,000-10,000 ft
Inactive: little rain, stratocumulus, light winds
Alternates between active and inactive phases

topic: Tropical Cyclone Tracks in the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Explain the origin and common location of the South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ).
concept: The SPCZ is a semi-permanent band of cloud, convergence, and precipitation that extends from the ITCZ/Coral Sea in a southeast direction across the tropical SW Pacific. It forms at the confluence of the trade winds and the mid-latitude westerlies, enhanced by the warm ocean temperatures of the SW Pacific. The SPCZ is most active and furthest south in the SH summer (December-April) and retreats north in SH winter. It stretches from approximately the Solomon Islands to Samoa and beyond toward French Polynesia. The SPCZ is a significant rainfall source for Pacific Island nations and influences weather as far south as NZ.
exam_tip: SPCZ: semi-permanent convergence band from Coral Sea SE through tropical SW Pacific. Most active SH summer (Dec-Apr). Significant rain source for Pacific islands. Influences NZ.
key_facts: Semi-permanent band of convergence and precipitation
Extends from Coral Sea SE through tropical SW Pacific
Forms at confluence of trade winds and westerlies
Enhanced by warm SW Pacific ocean temperatures
Most active SH summer (December to April)
Retreats north in SH winter (June-August)
Significant rainfall source for Pacific Island nations

syllabus_desc: Describe weather associated with the SPCZ.
concept: Weather associated with the SPCZ: (1) Deep convective cloud systems and cumulonimbus. (2) Heavy rainfall, sometimes persistent for days. (3) Squall lines along the convergence zone. (4) Strong gusty winds in squalls, light winds between. (5) High cloud (cirrus) extending ahead of the SPCZ. (6) Moderate to severe turbulence in active convective areas. (7) Significant icing in cloud. (8) For NZ aviation: when the SPCZ extends far south, it can contribute to sub-tropical moisture and cloud spreading over the north of NZ, particularly the North Island, producing persistent cloud and rain.
exam_tip: SPCZ weather: deep Cb, heavy rain, squall lines, gusty winds. For NZ: when far south, sub-tropical moisture and cloud spread over North Island.
key_facts: Deep convective cloud systems (Cb, MCS)
Heavy and persistent rainfall
Squall lines along the convergence zone
Gusty, squally winds within active zones
High cloud (Ci) extending ahead
Moderate to severe turbulence in active areas
NZ impact: sub-tropical cloud and rain over North Island when SPCZ is far south

syllabus_desc: With regard to the Trade Winds, describe the: mechanisms that drive the Trade Winds.
concept: The trade winds are driven by the Hadley cell circulation: air subsides at the subtropical high (~25-30 degrees), creating high surface pressure; pressure gradient drives air toward the lower pressure of the equatorial trough; as air flows toward the equator, Coriolis deflects it to the LEFT in the SH (creating SE trade winds) and to the RIGHT in the NH (creating NE trade winds). The strength of the trade winds is proportional to the pressure gradient between the subtropical high and the equatorial trough.
exam_tip: Trade winds driven by: Hadley cell subsidence at subtropical high + pressure gradient toward equatorial trough + Coriolis deflection (SE in SH, NE in NH).
key_facts: Hadley cell subsidence creates subtropical high-pressure belt
Pressure gradient from subtropical high toward equatorial trough
Air flows toward lower pressure (equatorial trough)
Coriolis deflects: SE in Southern Hemisphere
Coriolis deflects: NE in Northern Hemisphere
Strength proportional to subtropical high/equatorial trough pressure difference

syllabus_desc: With regard to the Trade Winds, describe the: approximate latitudinal and vertical limits.
concept: Latitudinal limits: Trade winds extend from the equatorial trough (approximately 5 degrees N/S) to the subtropical high belt (approximately 25-30 degrees N/S). Vertical limits: the trade winds are a low-level phenomenon, confined to the lowest 1,500-2,000 m (5,000-6,500 ft) of the atmosphere. Above the trade winds, a temperature inversion (the trade wind inversion) caps the moist, cooler trade wind layer below and warmer, drier air above. Above the inversion, the return flow of the Hadley cell moves poleward at high altitude.
exam_tip: Trade wind latitudes: 5 degrees to 25-30 degrees each hemisphere. Vertical: below ~1,500-2,000 m. Capped by trade wind inversion. Warmer dry air above the inversion.
key_facts: Latitudinal limits: equatorial trough (~5 degrees) to subtropical high (~25-30 degrees)
Vertical limits: below approximately 1,500-2,000 m (5,000-6,500 ft)
Capped by the trade wind inversion
Trade wind inversion: warm dry air above, cooler moist air below
Above the inversion: Hadley return flow (poleward)

syllabus_desc: With regard to the Trade Winds, describe the: seasonal location and direction.
concept: Direction: SE trade winds in SH, NE trade winds in NH. Seasonal location: the trade wind belt migrates seasonally with the ITCZ. In SH summer (Dec-Feb), the SE trade winds extend further south and the belt contracts north. In SH winter (Jun-Aug), the SE trades are strongest and most consistent, extending to about 30 degrees S. The NE and SE trade wind belts meet at the ITCZ, whose position shifts with the season.
exam_tip: SE trades (SH), NE trades (NH). Belt shifts seasonally with ITCZ. SH winter (Jun-Aug): SE trades strongest, most consistent, extending to ~30 S.
key_facts: SE trade winds in Southern Hemisphere
NE trade winds in Northern Hemisphere
Both converge at the ITCZ
Belt migrates seasonally with the ITCZ
SH winter: SE trades strongest and most consistent
SH summer: trade belt shifts north, less consistent in SH

syllabus_desc: With regard to the Trade Winds, describe the: commonly associated weather.
concept: Weather in the trade winds: on windward sides of islands, heavy orographic rainfall and low cloud. In the open ocean trade wind zone: fair weather cumulus (trade wind cumulus) capped at the trade wind inversion, excellent visibility, moderate steady winds 10-25 kts, occasional brief showers. The trade wind inversion produces a well-defined cloud base at about 2,000 ft. Periods of more active weather occur when trade wind disturbances (waves in the easterlies) propagate westward through the trade wind belt.
exam_tip: Trade wind weather: windward island = heavy orographic rain. Open ocean = fair weather Cu capped at inversion, excellent visibility, 10-25 kt steady winds.
key_facts: Windward island sides: heavy orographic rainfall, low cloud
Leeward island sides: dry, sunny
Open ocean: fair-weather cumulus (trade wind Cu)
Cloud capped at trade wind inversion (~2,000 ft)
Excellent visibility in open ocean trades
Steady 10-25 kt winds
Occasional enhanced activity from easterly waves

syllabus_desc: With regard to the Trade Winds, describe the: winds and weather usually experienced above the Trade Winds.
concept: Above the trade wind inversion: warm, dry air with few clouds. The upper-level return flow of the Hadley cell moves poleward at this level. Winds shift direction with altitude - at the surface SE trades, above the inversion the flow transitions to more westerly or variable directions. High cirrus may be present from distant deep convection. Clear skies and excellent visibility above the inversion. The trade wind inversion itself is often a thin but clearly defined layer of strong temperature increase with altitude.
exam_tip: Above trade wind inversion: warm dry air, few clouds, poleward return flow, westerly component. Clear and excellent visibility. High Ci from distant convection.
key_facts: Warm, dry air above the trade wind inversion
Few clouds above the inversion
Poleward Hadley return flow at altitude
Wind direction shifts from SE surface to more westerly above
Clear skies, excellent visibility
Occasional cirrus from distant deep convection

topic: Cyclone Hazard — Sea State and Storm Surge
syllabus_desc: With regard to the Trade Winds, describe the: topographical influences on the Trade Winds.
concept: Topography significantly modifies trade winds around Pacific islands: windward sides receive heavy orographic rain and cloud; leeward sides are dry and sunny (rain shadow effect). Gaps between islands or through mountain ranges accelerate the trade winds (gap winds). Islands can generate sea breeze circulations that interact with and modify the trades. Strong trades over rough terrain generate significant mechanical turbulence. Isolated island peaks above the trade wind inversion protrude into the dry upper air and experience different wind conditions from lowland areas.
exam_tip: Topography and trade winds: windward = heavy rain, leeward = dry rain shadow. Gap winds accelerate through gaps. Island sea breezes interact with trades. Turbulence over rough terrain.
key_facts: Windward side: heavy orographic rain and cloud
Leeward side: dry rain shadow - sunny and dry
Gap winds: trades accelerate through gaps between islands
Island sea breezes interact with and modify trade winds
Mechanical turbulence over rough terrain
High island peaks protrude through trade wind inversion into dry upper air

syllabus_desc: Define 'monsoon'.
concept: The monsoon is a seasonal reversal of the prevailing wind direction driven by differential heating between land and ocean. In summer, the land heats more rapidly than the ocean, creating a thermal low over the land and a pressure gradient from ocean to land - moist onshore winds blow (wet monsoon). In winter, the land cools faster, creating high pressure over land and a pressure gradient from land to ocean - dry offshore winds blow (dry monsoon). The Asian summer monsoon is the most significant example. The Australian monsoon (wet season) brings heavy rainfall to northern Australia from November to April as the ITCZ migrates south.
exam_tip: Monsoon: seasonal wind reversal due to land/ocean temperature contrast. Summer: land heats faster = onshore moist wet monsoon. Winter: land cools faster = offshore dry monsoon.
key_facts: Seasonal reversal of prevailing wind direction
Driven by differential heating: land vs ocean
Summer (wet monsoon): land heats faster, thermal low, onshore moist wind
Winter (dry monsoon): land cools faster, high pressure, offshore dry wind
Asian monsoon: world's most significant example
Australian wet season: ITCZ migrates south, Nov-April

syllabus_desc: Describe the mechanisms involved with regard to wet monsoons.
concept: The wet monsoon develops through: (1) Summer solar heating creates intense thermal lows over the land (Indian subcontinent, northern Australia). (2) The thermal low draws in moist maritime air from the ocean. (3) The onshore flow brings warm, humid air that rises over the land, generating deep convection, heavy rainfall, and thunderstorms. (4) The ITCZ migrates toward the heated land, further enhancing convergence and rainfall. (5) Latent heat released in the deep convection reinforces the monsoon circulation, creating a self-sustaining feedback. (6) The upper-level outflow diverges over the ocean, completing the circulation.
exam_tip: Wet monsoon: intense land heating (thermal low) draws moist ocean air inland. ITCZ migrates toward land. Deep convection, heavy rain. Latent heat feedback sustains circulation.
key_facts: Intense summer heating creates thermal low over land
Thermal low draws moist maritime air onshore
Warm humid onshore air rises over land
Deep convection and heavy rainfall develop
ITCZ migrates toward heated land mass
Latent heat released reinforces the monsoon circulation (feedback)
Upper-level outflow over ocean completes the circulation

topic: Tropical Cyclone Effects on New Zealand
syllabus_desc: State the seasons during which the Australian monsoons occur.
concept: The Australian monsoon (wet season) occurs during the Southern Hemisphere summer: November to April, with the peak in January-February. During this time the ITCZ migrates south over northern Australia, drawing in moist NW winds from the Timor Sea and Coral Sea. The dry season (dry monsoon, SE trades) occurs from May to October. The transition months (October-November and April-May) are the most unsettled. Northern Australian cities (Darwin, Broome, Cairns) experience pronounced wet and dry seasons driven by the monsoon.
exam_tip: Australian monsoon (wet): November to April (SH summer), peak January-February. ITCZ migrates south, NW moist winds over N Australia. Dry season: May-October.
key_facts: Wet season (Australian monsoon): November to April
Peak: January to February
SH summer - ITCZ migrates south over northern Australia
Moist NW winds from Timor Sea and Coral Sea
Dry season: May to October (SE trades)
Transition: October-November and April-May (unsettled)
Affects Darwin, Broome, Cairns prominently

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements for the formation and development of tropical cyclones.
concept: Tropical cyclone formation requirements: (1) Warm ocean - sea surface temperature (SST) >26 degrees C to a depth of at least 50 m (provides energy through evaporation and latent heat). (2) Latitude - at least 5 degrees from the equator (Coriolis force needed to initiate rotation). (3) Pre-existing disturbance - an initial area of disturbed weather (easterly wave, ITCZ cluster) to provide the rotation seed. (4) Low vertical wind shear - strong wind shear decapitates the developing storm. (5) Moist mid-levels - dry air entrainment suppresses convection. (6) Upper-level divergence to remove rising air at the storm top.
exam_tip: TC formation: SST >26C (>=50 m deep), >5 degrees from equator (Coriolis), pre-existing disturbance, low wind shear, moist mid-levels, upper divergence.
key_facts: SST >26 degrees C (to depth of 50 m)
At least 5 degrees latitude from equator (Coriolis force needed)
Pre-existing atmospheric disturbance (easterly wave, ITCZ cluster)
Low vertical wind shear (strong shear decapitates storm)
Moist mid-levels (dry air suppresses convection)
Upper-level divergence to evacuate rising air
All conditions must be present simultaneously

syllabus_desc: Describe the weather conditions associated with tropical cyclones.
concept: Weather in a tropical cyclone: (1) Eye: calm, warm, cloud-free or few clouds, light winds. Pressure at its lowest. (2) Eyewall: most intense zone - extreme winds (>64 kts category 1, up to 150+ kts severe), extreme turbulence, extreme icing, heavy rain reducing visibility to zero, storm surge. (3) Rainbands: spiral bands of deep Cb extending outward - severe to extreme turbulence and rain, intermittent between bands. (4) Outflow: high level cirrus spreading outward from the storm (up to 1,000 km). Storm surge: sea level rises 2-10 m near and ahead of storm track. Aviation: absolutely no flight within 100 nm of a TC centre.
exam_tip: TC weather: Eye = calm/clear. Eyewall = extreme wind/turbulence/rain/icing. Rainbands = severe turbulence. Storm surge 2-10 m. NO flight within 100 nm.
key_facts: Eye: calm, warm, light winds, cloud-free centre
Eyewall: extreme winds (>64 kts to 150+ kts), extreme turbulence
Eyewall: extreme icing, torrential rain, near-zero visibility
Rainbands: spiral Cb bands - severe turbulence and heavy rain
Outflow: high cirrus spreading up to 1,000 km from centre
Storm surge: 2-10 m sea level rise
Aviation: NO flight within 100 nm of TC centre

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors causing: El Nino events.
concept: El Nino events occur when the normal trade wind pattern in the Pacific weakens or reverses. Normally, SE trade winds pile warm water against Australia/western Pacific. In El Nino: the trade winds weaken or reverse; warm water moves eastward toward South America; sea surface temperatures in the central and eastern Pacific rise anomalously; the Walker circulation reverses direction. Causes are complex (coupled ocean-atmosphere system) but are triggered by initial weakening of trade winds, possibly from internal variability. El Nino events typically develop from mid-year and peak in December-January.
exam_tip: El Nino: trade winds weaken/reverse, warm Pacific water shifts east toward South America, Walker circulation reverses. Typical peak: December-January.
key_facts: Normal trade winds weaken or reverse direction
Warm water shifts from western to central/eastern Pacific
Sea surface temperature rises in central/eastern Pacific
Walker circulation weakens and may reverse
Typically develops from mid-year, peaks December-January
Coupled ocean-atmosphere feedback sustains the event
Recurs every 2-7 years on average

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors causing: La Nina events.
concept: La Nina is the opposite phase of ENSO (El Nino-Southern Oscillation). In La Nina: the SE trade winds strengthen above normal; warm water is pushed further west than normal; SSTs in the eastern Pacific become unusually cool; the Walker circulation intensifies. La Nina typically brings enhanced rainfall to Australia, Indonesia, and the western Pacific, while the eastern Pacific is drier than normal. For NZ, La Nina tends to bring more northerly/NE winds with warmer temperatures in the north and increased chance of tropical cyclones affecting NZ.
exam_tip: La Nina: opposite of El Nino. Trade winds strengthen, warm water pushed further west, eastern Pacific cools. Enhanced rain W Pacific/Australia. NZ: more northerly winds, cyclone risk.
key_facts: SE trade winds strengthen above normal
Warm water pushed further west than normal
Eastern Pacific sea surface temperatures cool
Walker circulation intensifies
Enhanced rainfall: Australia, Indonesia, western Pacific
Eastern Pacific drier than normal
NZ: more northerly/NE winds, warmer north, higher cyclone risk

topic: Aviation Weather Services for Tropical Operations
syllabus_desc: Describe how El Nino and La Nina events influence the weather in New Zealand.
concept: El Nino effects on NZ: increased SW winds; drier and cooler than average in the north and east; drought risk in Northland/eastern regions; reduced tropical cyclone activity in the SW Pacific near NZ; more frequent southerly outbreaks in southern NZ; reduced rainfall in some regions. La Nina effects on NZ: increased NE/N winds; warmer and wetter in the north and northeast; increased chance of tropical cyclone remnants tracking into NZ; drought in southern/western NZ; enhanced rainfall in northern NZ. ENSO effects are probabilistic - not deterministic - and other patterns (e.g. SAM, IPO) also influence NZ weather.
exam_tip: El Nino NZ: SW winds, drier/cooler N/E, fewer cyclones. La Nina NZ: NE winds, warmer/wetter north, more cyclone remnants affecting NZ.
key_facts: El Nino NZ: increased SW winds
El Nino NZ: drier and cooler in north and east
El Nino NZ: drought risk in Northland and eastern regions
El Nino NZ: reduced tropical cyclone activity near NZ
La Nina NZ: increased NE/N winds
La Nina NZ: warmer and wetter in northern NZ
La Nina NZ: increased chance of tropical cyclone remnants reaching NZ
ENSO effects are probabilistic, not deterministic

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, using given examples of satellite imagery, identify the following: areas of stable and unstable air.
concept: Stable air on satellite imagery: smooth, uniform, layered cloud textures (grey, continuous); well-defined stratiform cloud sheets; cloud edges aligned with isobars; no vertical development evident. Unstable air on satellite imagery: bright white (tall, deep cloud); cauliflower or lumpy texture (Cb tops); cellular or honeycomb patterns (open cells indicate instability); clustered bright areas with sharp edges; anvil cirrus spreading from cloud tops. Dark (cloud-free) areas indicate subsiding stable air (anticyclone). Deep bright cloud masses indicate active convection.
exam_tip: Stable air satellite: smooth grey layered cloud, uniform, cloud-free in highs. Unstable: bright white (tall Cb), lumpy/cauliflower texture, cellular patterns.
key_facts: Stable air: smooth grey layered cloud (stratiform)
Stable air: well-defined cloud sheets aligned with isobars
Stable air: dark cloud-free areas (subsiding highs)
Unstable air: bright white cloud (tall, deep, cold tops)
Unstable air: lumpy/cauliflower texture (Cb tops)
Unstable air: cellular or honeycomb cloud patterns
Unstable air: clustered bright cloud masses with sharp edges

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, using given examples of satellite imagery, identify the following: the processes causing each significant area or mass of cloud.
concept: Cloud processes identifiable on satellite: (1) Frontal cloud - elongated bands aligned with surface fronts; band width and brightness indicates frontal intensity. (2) Orographic cloud - cloud lines or caps aligned with mountain ranges (N-S in NZ Southern Alps). (3) Convective clusters - isolated bright cells or MCS over warm ocean or heated land. (4) Anticyclonic subsidence - spiral-patterned stratocumulus sheets in Sc lines (cloud streets). (5) Frontal occlusion - a complex comma-shaped cloud pattern. (6) SPCZ/ITCZ - broad convergence band visible as continuous cloud.
exam_tip: Satellite cloud processes: frontal bands (elongated), orographic caps (mountain-aligned), convective clusters (bright cells), cloud streets in highs, comma occlusion, ITCZ/SPCZ bands.
key_facts: Frontal cloud: elongated bands, width/brightness = intensity
Orographic cloud: aligned with mountain ranges
Convective clusters: isolated bright cells (Cb) or MCS
Cloud streets: Sc rolls in anticyclone, aligned with wind
Occluded front: comma-shaped cloud pattern
ITCZ/SPCZ: broad continuous convergence cloud band

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, using given examples of satellite imagery, identify the following: likely cloud types and weather associated with each significant area of cloud.
concept: Cloud types and weather from satellite: Bright white, very cold tops (IR) = Cb/Cumulonimbus - thunderstorms, severe turbulence, heavy rain, hail, icing. Smooth grey stratus = St/Sc - low cloud, drizzle, IFR conditions. Grey sheets with wavy texture = Ns/As - continuous rain, moderate turbulence, icing. Lumpy white cells = TCu/Large Cu - showers, moderate turbulence. Thin filaments/sheets = Ci/Cs - high cloud, possible halo, weather deteriorating (warm front approaching). Cellular patterns = open cell Sc - showers in shower areas.
exam_tip: Satellite cloud type/weather: Bright white (very cold IR) = Cb (storms). Grey smooth = St/Sc (low cloud, drizzle). Grey sheets = Ns/As (continuous rain). Thin filaments = Ci/Cs (warm front).
key_facts: Bright white very cold IR tops = Cumulonimbus - severe weather
Smooth grey = Stratus/Sc - low cloud, drizzle, IFR
Grey layered sheets = Ns/As - continuous rain, icing
Lumpy white cells = TCu - showers, moderate turbulence
Thin white filaments/veil = Ci/Cs - warm front approaching
Cellular open patterns = shower clouds with clear air between

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, interpret radar imagery in terms of: precipitation types and intensity causing the radar echo.
concept: Radar echo intensity: measured in dBZ (decibels of reflectivity). Light rain: 20-35 dBZ (green on most displays). Moderate rain: 35-45 dBZ (yellow). Heavy rain: 45-55 dBZ (red). Extreme rain/hail: >55 dBZ (purple/magenta). Dry snow: lower reflectivity than rain of same water content. Hail: highest reflectivity (three-body scatter spike). The bright band: a layer of enhanced reflectivity at the 0 degrees C level where snowflakes partially melt and reflect strongly (melting layer). Radar detects water-coated particles best - ice crystals give weak returns.
exam_tip: Radar reflectivity (dBZ): light rain = 20-35 (green), moderate = 35-45 (yellow), heavy = 45-55 (red), extreme/hail >55 (purple). Bright band at 0 C level.
key_facts: Light rain: 20-35 dBZ (green)
Moderate rain: 35-45 dBZ (yellow)
Heavy rain: 45-55 dBZ (red)
Extreme rain/hail: >55 dBZ (purple/magenta)
Bright band: enhanced reflectivity at 0 degrees C (melting layer)
Hail: highest reflectivity readings
Dry snow and ice crystals: weaker returns than equivalent liquid

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, interpret radar imagery in terms of: likely cloud types associated with the precipitation echo.
concept: Cloud types from radar echoes: Isolated intense cells (high dBZ, limited horizontal extent): Cumulonimbus - embedded or visible Cb, severe weather. Extensive areas of moderate dBZ: Nimbostratus/Altostratus - frontal rain. Scattered moderate cells: Cumulus congestus or TCu - showery pattern. Linear band of high dBZ: cold front squall line. Stratiform rain with bright band: Nimbostratus. Scattered cells in clear background: shower activity (Sc or Cu). Very extensive low dBZ: widespread drizzle from Stratus.
exam_tip: Radar/cloud type: isolated intense cells = Cb (severe). Extensive moderate = Ns (frontal rain). Bright band = Ns. Linear band = cold front squall. Scattered cells = Sc/Cu showers.
key_facts: Isolated intense cells (high dBZ) = Cumulonimbus
Extensive moderate dBZ with bright band = Nimbostratus
Widespread low dBZ = Stratus (drizzle)
Linear high dBZ band = cold front squall line
Scattered moderate cells = shower pattern (Sc/Cu)
Bright band (enhanced layer) = melting zone below Ns

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, interpret radar imagery in terms of: speed of movement and timing of radar echoes, and the expected impact at given locations.
concept: Movement and timing: radar echoes move with the steering wind (approximately 500-700 hPa wind). Extrapolating echo position: estimate time of arrival at a location by measuring current position, movement direction and speed. Example: echo 100 nm NW moving SE at 30 kts will arrive in approximately 3 hours 20 minutes. Radar limitations: range (NZ MetService radar range 200-250 km); beam overshoots low-level shallow precipitation at long range; attenuation behind heavy rain hides cells; no Doppler = no rotation detection on standard radar. Use multiple radar updates to confirm echo movement direction.
exam_tip: Radar timing: echoes move with steering wind (500-700 hPa). Extrapolate: distance / speed = arrival time. Limitations: 200-250 km range, beam overshoot, attenuation, no rotation.
key_facts: Echoes move with steering wind (approximately 500-700 hPa)
Extrapolate: measure distance and divide by echo speed = ETA
Example: 100 nm at 30 kts = ~3 hr 20 min arrival
Use multiple scans to confirm movement direction
Range limit: NZ radar covers ~200-250 km
Beam overshoot: misses shallow low-level precipitation at long range
Attenuation: heavy rain hides echoes behind it
Multiple updates needed to confirm echo movement
